question: Is the statement supported by the evidence?
answer: FINAL ANSWER: Below is a concise, action‑focused summary of what the records show about each of your eight symptoms, the most likely causes, immediate checks to run now, and the recommended ticket classification / next steps. I kept each evidence statement tied to the incident records we have. Executive summary - The pattern of simultaneous D365/reporting, Exchange/Outlook send, Teams/voice (Zurich one‑way, Basel ~30s drops, new numbers failing) and printer failures matches a weekend change that affected multiple domains (perimeter/firewall/NAT, SBC/Direct‑Routing, IIS/TLS or VM restore/OS changes, and AD/GPO/print‑server state). Treat this as a cross‑domain production outage (Major/P1) and escalate to 2nd‑level engineering (Network/UC/Platform/Identity/Print) for coordinated response and rollback testing
context: SOURCE 23: ﻿ Ticket summary: Ticket name: KF52: Microsoft Teams – External calls without audio (only silence) Ticket number: TI-1602241 Company: Customer Company 52 AG Contact person: Contact Person 14 Owner: Employee 33 Origin: Phone Type: Incident Created on: 08.07.25 08:21 Closure Closure date: 18.07.25 16:05S Closure text: Analysis completed. Configuration adjusted according to Direct Routing architecture. Voice transmission stable again. Activities 08.07.25 08:10 Phone notification. Several employees report: • External calls can be established • The other party can hear them • They themselves hear nothing (complete silence) Internal Teams-to-Teams calls function. Chat and video also function. Affected: approx. 12 users at the Zurich location. 08.07.25 08:21 Ticket created Assigned to: Employee 33 Status: In Progress Notes 08.07.25 09:05 Helpdesk asks: • Does it affect only external PSTN calls? • Only incoming or also outgoing? • Headset or speaker? • WLAN or LAN? 08.07.25 09:30 Customer responds: • Only external calls affected • Incoming and outgoing • Various headsets • LAN connection • Other locations not affected 08.07.25 10:15 Helpdesk tests with affected user: Test 1 – Internal Teams call → OK Test 2 – External mobile call → No audio Test 3 – External landline call → No audio Notable: Call status shows “Connected”. 08.07.25 11:40 Initial hypothesis: Client issue Measures: • Teams cache cleared • Teams reinstalled • Drivers checked Result: No change. 09.07.25 08:55 Customer adds: A new firewall rule was implemented at the location over the weekend (Security Hardening). Unclear whether there is a correlation. 09.07.25 10:10 Helpdesk checks: • Internet stable • No packet loss visible • Microsoft 365 status normal Suspicion: Network / VoIP layer Ticket forwarded to: BU Unified Communications 09.07.25 10:22 Ticket taken over by: System Engineer 18 In-depth analysis 09.07.25 11:15 Analysis in Teams Admin Center: • Direct Routing active • SBC status: Online • PSTN calls are signaled • No errors in SIP signaling Notable: • RTP stream appears not to run bidirectionally 10.07.25 08:40 SBC log analysis: • SIP INVITE correct • 200 OK correct • Media negotiation (SDP) successful RTP stream from Microsoft IP visible RTP stream from internal client not consistently visible 10.07.25 10:50 Firewall logs reviewed. New rule implemented: Outbound traffic restricted Only defined port ranges allowed Documentation shows: Teams media ports UDP 3478–3481 and dynamic RTP ports 49152–53247 Firewall rule allows only 3478–3481. 11.07.25 09:15 Test: Temporary opening of wider UDP port range → Audio works immediately. Port restricted again → No audio. 11.07.25 13:20 Comparison with internal architecture documentation: Document: “Blueprint – Microsoft Teams Direct Routing & Media Flow – Version 2.1” Section 3 describes: • Media Bypass behavior • RTP port range requirements • Location-specific firewall rules • NAT traversal 14.07.25 Comparison shows: Zurich location uses Media Bypass Other locations do not. With Media Bypass, RTP runs directly from client to SBC. Firewall rule was designed only for signaling. 16.07.25 Configuration adjustment carried out according to architecture specification. Details not documented in the ticket for security reasons. 18.07.25 15:30 Multiple external test calls successful. Audio stable in both directions. Monitoring 48h without anomalies. Ticket closure 18.07.25 16:05 Dear Contact Person 14 The root cause was a restriction of media port communication following a firewall adjustment at the Zurich location. The configuration was adjusted according to the internal Direct Routing architecture. Voice transmission is stable again. Kind regards Consulting Sample Company SOURCE 16: ﻿ Ticket summary: Ticket name: KF52: Microsoft Teams – External calls disconnect after approx. 30 seconds Ticket number: TI-1604879 Company: Customer Company 52 AG Contact person: Contact Person 16 Owner: Employee 35 Origin: Email Type: Incident Created on: 22.07.25 09:14 Closure Closure date: 31.07.25 15:52 Closure text: Analysis completed. Adjustment made according to Direct Routing architecture. Calls stable. Activities 22.07.25 09:02 – Email from Contact Person 16 to Helpdesk Good morning Since yesterday, several employees report that external calls in Microsoft Teams disconnect after approximately 30 seconds. The call is established normally, both parties can hear each other, but the connection is terminated after a short time. Internal Teams calls are not affected. Could you please review this? Kind regards 22.07.25 09:14 Ticket created and assigned to Employee 35. 22.07.25 10:05 – Helpdesk inquiry Good day Thank you for your report. Could you please confirm: – Does it affect incoming and outgoing calls? – Does the disconnection always occur after approximately the same duration? – Are all locations affected or only a specific one? Thank you very much. 22.07.25 10:42 – Customer response It only affects the Basel location. Both incoming and outgoing calls. The disconnection occurs almost exactly after 30 seconds, sometimes slightly earlier. Home office users are not affected. 22.07.25 11:30 – Internal note Test performed with affected user. External call to mobile phone. Call disconnected after 29–31 seconds. No error message in the Teams client, simply “Call Ended.” 23.07.25 08:55 – Initial hypothesis Possible client error or unstable connection. Test with another device → same behavior. Test with LAN cable instead of docking station → no change. 23.07.25 11:10 – Email to customer Good day We are currently analyzing the Direct Routing logs. Signaling is established correctly. The disconnection does not appear to be triggered by the client. Were there any recent network changes at the Basel location? Kind regards 23.07.25 13:45 – Customer response On Friday, a new network segment for VoIP traffic was implemented. A separate VLAN for “Voice” was created. Could this be relevant? 24.07.25 09:05 – Internal analysis SBC logs reviewed: – SIP INVITE successful – 200 OK correct – Call established – After 30 seconds, BYE from SBC No obvious error code. 24.07.25 10:30 – Firewall analysis No blocking visible. Ports open according to standard configuration. 24.07.25 14:20 – Detailed SIP analysis Session-Expires header reviewed. Session timer set to 30 seconds. Comparison with other locations shows: There, 180 seconds. 25.07.25 08:50 – Review of internal documentation Document: “Blueprint – Microsoft Teams Direct Routing & Session Handling – Version 2.1” Section on NAT session persistence and SIP session timers relevant. Note in the blueprint: For certain firewall or NAT configurations, the session timer must be adjusted to avoid premature termination. 25.07.25 11:15 – Internal tests Temporary adjustment of session parameters on SBC. Test call performed. Call remains stable for several minutes. Reverting to original configuration → disconnection after approx. 30 seconds reproducible. 28.07.25 – Coordination with network team New “Voice” VLAN uses different NAT policy. UDP session timeout configured to 30 seconds. Comparison with Zurich location → 180 seconds. 29.07.25 – Adjustment NAT session timeout adjusted according to architecture specification. Details not documented in ticket for security reasons. 30.07.25 – Customer feedback Good morning We conducted several test calls. No more disconnections observed. Longer conversations are also stable. Thank you very much. 31.07.25 15:52 – Ticket closure Dear Contact Person 16 The root cause was a deviation in the session and NAT configuration at the Basel location related to the new VLAN structure. The configuration was adjusted according to the internal Direct Routing architecture. Calls now remain stable. Kind regards Consulting Sample Company REPORT 55: # TI-722511 — Financial Reports outage (AOS/IIS TLS cipher-suite change) Ticket TI-722511 documents a reproducible outage where users (EMEA finance) could not open Financial Reports in both UAT and PROD; the incident was triaged with a consulting partner and resolved by activating best-practice TLS cipher suites using IISCRYPTO and restarting AOS (and IIS) servers. Key infrastructure components implicated are AOS servers and IIS servers (TLS/cipher negotiation), TLS policy/configuration and Windows security updates as potential contributors; an internal JIRA (ICO261-183) from another region reported the same error, indicating a global or multi-region manifestation that requires coordinated configuration and testing across environments. Ownership and operational actions were recorded in portal notes (contact persons and consulting company involvement), and the ticket closure explicitly links the TLS/cipher change and server restarts to the remediation outcome. ## TI-722511 is a production-impacting, multi-environment incident resolved by TLS/cipher-suite changes and server restarts The ticket TI-722511 records reproducible errors accessing Financial Reports across UAT and PROD and documents closure text indicating that best-practice cipher suites were activated with IISCRYPTO and AOS servers were restarted as the remediation that resolved the issue. This linkage between the ticket, the tool (IISCRYPTO), the configuration change and the server restarts is explicitly documented in the closure notes and portal history [Data: Entities (0); Entities (18,20); Relationships (9,34,36)]. Because the ticket affects both UAT and PROD and includes symptom reports from EMEA finance users (report creators appearing as "unknown" and inability to open reports), this is a production-grade operational incident rather than a single-user problem; the ticket metadata and relationships show cross-environment impact and operator action items that are relevant to architecture and runbook updates [Data: Entities (0); Relationships (1,10)]. ## AOS and IIS servers are central operational dependencies for the reporting service AOS servers broker requests from user clients (browser/Report Designer) to backend reporting services and were explicitly restarted as part of the remediation, showing they are a required operational touchpoint when TLS/cipher changes are applied. The relationships document the dependency between AOS servers, users and TLS policy changes, and record that AOS restarts were necessary after cipher adjustments [Data: Entities (19,483,487); Relationships (796,798,36)]. IIS servers are also the target of IISCRYPTO changes and therefore form the TLS termination layer that directly affects client report access; the record linking IISCRYPTO to IIS servers and to the best-practice cipher-suite configuration confirms they are part of the change domain that produced the outage [Data: Entities (26,18,20); Relationships (35,34)]. ## TLS / cipher-suite configuration and Windows security updates are a plausible root-cause vector and must be part of diagnostics The dataset explicitly identifies TLS/cipher-suite configuration as a primary decision-tree item for multi-environment report failures and calls out that Windows security updates can alter available crypto libraries or TLS behavior, impacting negotiation and compatibility [Data: Entities (480,488); Relationships (803)]. Operationally this means any change to the TLS configuration (cipher suites or minimum TLS versions) or a cumulative OS/security patch may introduce incompatibilities between client/report-designer, IIS, AOS and backend services; the incident remediation (activating best-practice ciphers and restarting servers) and the diagnostic checklist in the records both point to these items as the first-line troubleshooting steps [Data: Entities (0,20,480,488); Relationships (9,803,36)]. ## The incident shows cross-regional recurrence — internal Jira ICO261-183 reports the same error An internal JIRA ticket (ICO261-183) opened by another internal team (Mexico) reported the same error, and the portal notes for TI-722511 reference that JIRA ticket, indicating the problem was observed outside a single operating region and should be treated as a global/tenant-level issue requiring coordinated remediation and communication [Data: Entities (17); Relationships (33); Entities (0); Relationships (0)]. This cross-reference implies the root cause is likely systemic (configuration or policy across environments) rather than an isolated host-level fault, elevating the need for a deployment-wide review of TLS/cipher policies, synchronized change windows, and shared runbooks to avoid reoccurrence [Data: Entities (17,0); Relationships (33,9)]. ## Consulting company acted as primary triage/coordination but operational ownership and escalation paths are fragmented Portal no-reply entries and the ticket relationships identify a consulting organization performing triage, posting portal updates and coordinating remediation activities for TI-722511, which indicates third-party involvement in operational execution [Data: Entities (24); Relationships (16)]. While external support can accelerate remediation, the records show multiple actors (customer contact, consulting company, internal teams, and referenced JIRA) are involved; this multiplicity increases coordination overhead and suggests the need to codify explicit escalation and handoff steps (who is authorized to change TLS settings, who validates post-change testing, and who owns cross-region communication) to reduce confusion during future incidents [Data: Entities (2,24,17); Relationships (1,16,33)]. ## The ticket closure documents a fix but there is limited explicit trace of pre- and post-change testing and rollback procedures Closure text states best-practice cipher suites were activated with IISCRYPTO and servers restarted, and this outcome is associated with resolution of the ticket, but the dataset does not include detailed pre-change compatibility testing results, a staged rollout record, or an explicit rollback plan linked to the change [Data: Entities (0,18,20); Relationships (9,34)]. For production TLS changes that can break client compatibility, the absence of documented staged testing or rollback guidance in the ticket and relationships represents an operational risk — future changes should include mandatory compatibility validation (Report Designer and browser tests across supported clients), versioned configuration snapshots, and an approved rollback procedure recorded in the change ticket before applying IISCRYPTO or similar tooling [Data: Entities (0,480,18); Relationships (9,34,35)]. ## Recommended immediate actions: formalize TLS change control, add AOS/IIS restart playbook, and update monitoring/alerts Given the documented remediation and dependencies, the highest-priority operational actions are: 1) formalize a TLS/cipher-suite change control process that enforces synchronized configuration across UAT and PROD and requires pre-change validation; 2) add a concise runbook for TLS changes that includes steps to use IISCRYPTO, how and when to restart IIS and AOS servers, and post-change verification for report access; and 3) tune monitoring/alerts to detect report designer failures, unknown report-owner symptoms, and TLS negotiation failures so teams can detect regressions quickly. These recommendations are directly derived from the incident facts that link TLS configuration, IISCRYPTO use, and server restarts to resolution [Data: Entities (0,18,20,19,26,480); Relationships (9,34,36,35,796)]. Implementing these controls will reduce time-to-detect and time-to-recover for similar incidents and make cross-team communication and escalation more predictable. ## Governance and long-term mitigation: standardize TLS baselines, document supported client profiles, and maintain a cross-region configuration registry The incident demonstrates the need for organizational governance around TLS baselines (approved cipher suites and minimum TLS versions) and explicit documentation of supported client versions for the Report Designer and browser clients. Records show the problem manifested across regions and environments and was tied to cipher-suite changes, reinforcing that a single source of truth for TLS configuration and supported client compatibility is required [Data: Entities (480,483); Relationships (803,796,36)]. A practical control is a configuration registry (or CMDB extension) that records TLS/cipher settings per environment and a pre-deployment verification checklist that gates any change; combine this with scheduled compatibility testing against a matrix of client versions to avoid breaking production users when adopting "best-practice" cryptographic settings that may remove older ciphers still required by legacy clients [Data: Entities (480,483); Relationships (36,796)]. ## Communication and escalation runbook: define stakeholder matrix and cross-reference internal tracking systems The records link the ticket to named customer contacts (Contact Person 7, Regina Jagsch), a consulting company, and an internal JIRA that reported the same error, indicating multiple stakeholders and tracking systems were in use during the incident [Data: Entities (2,11,24,17); Relationships (1,10,16,33)]. To streamline future incidents, create a short escalation runbook that maps roles (customer contact, platform-owner, consulting SPOC, security owner), primary communication channels, and which external/internal ticketing system must be updated (portal ticket and JIRA). Include templates for status updates and a requirement to escalate to platform engineering when TLS/cipher changes are proposed, ensuring a single coordinated change window and clear ownership for rollback and postmortem actions [Data: Entities (0,2,11,24,17); Relationships (1,10,16,33)].

answer: . What each symptom most likely means, and quick evidence 1) D365 Financial Reports / Report Designer: slow/failed loads, “unknown” authors, “report cannot be found” on both test and prod - Likely cause: TLS/cipher or IIS/AOS configuration drift between nodes (node‑specific TLS negotiation failure), or incomplete post‑change validation after a weekend cipher/TLS hardening. REPORT 141: # IIS Servers — TLS Cipher Hardening (IISCRYPTO & Best-practice Cipher Suites) This community centers on a security remediation that enabled best-practice TLS cipher suites on IIS web servers using the IISCRYPTO tool; the change (and associated server restarts) is documented as the resolution for ticket TI-722511. Key entities are the BEST-PRACTICE CIPHER SUITES configuration, the IISCRYPTO tooling used to apply it, and the IIS SERVERS that were the targets of the change. The records show a direct tooling→configuration→infrastructure chain: IISCRYPTO was used to activate the cipher configuration on IIS servers, and the ticket closure ties that configuration change to incident remediation. ## Ticket TI-722511 closed after activating best-practice cipher suites The incident ticket identified as TI-722511 was closed after the BEST-PRACTICE CIPHER SUITES configuration was activated; the closure notes explicitly attribute the configuration change as part of the fix [Data: Relationships (9); Entities (20)]. This establishes that the cipher-suite change was not merely advisory but was executed as an operational remediation tied to a tracked support item, indicating change control and an incident lifecycle touchpoint that should be captured in post-incident review and runbooks. ## IISCRYPTO is the documented tool used to implement the cipher changes Records show IISCRYPTO was used to activate the BEST-PRACTICE CIPHER SUITES configuration on servers — a direct tooling-to-configuration relationship recorded in closure notes [Data: Relationships (34); Entities (18), (20)]. Additionally, IISCRYPTO is explicitly associated with modifying IIS servers, confirming it is the operational mechanism for this change rather than a manual registry edit or alternative tool [Data: Relationships (35); Entities (18), (26)]. This identifies IISCRYPTO as a key operational control that should be included in configuration management, testing procedures, and any automation/orchestration workflows for TLS configuration. ## IIS servers are the target infrastructure and part of the web/application tier The IIS SERVERS entity is described as the target of the IISCRYPTO configuration changes and is noted as part of the web/application server tier alongside AOS servers [Data: Relationships (35); Entities (26)]. Because the IIS servers are in the application tier, changes to their TLS configuration have potential reach to client compatibility, web application availability, and upstream/downstream integration points; the records therefore identify these servers as an important operational domain for TLS policy enforcement and verification. ## Remediation included activation of recommended cipher suites and server restarts The BEST-PRACTICE CIPHER SUITES record documents that activation of recommended TLS cipher suites, performed with IISCRYPTO and accompanied by server restarts, resolved the issue described in the ticket [Data: Entities (20); Relationships (9); Relationships (34)]. REPORT 7: # KF11 Financial Reporting Community — Report Designer, AOS, IIS/TLS, Data Mart (Customer Company 11) This community centers on the Management Reporting / Financial Reporting stack used by Customer Company 11 (D365 Finance & Operations) and the support ecosystem that operates it (consulting partner, CRM portal, CRM Development, and Microsoft escalation). context: REPORT 55: # TI-722511 — Financial Reports outage (AOS/IIS TLS cipher-suite change) Ticket TI-722511 documents a reproducible outage where users (EMEA finance) could not open Financial Reports in both UAT and PROD; the incident was triaged with a consulting partner and resolved by activating best-practice TLS cipher suites using IISCRYPTO and restarting AOS (and IIS) servers. Key infrastructure components implicated are AOS servers and IIS servers (TLS/cipher negotiation), TLS policy/configuration and Windows security updates as potential contributors; an internal JIRA (ICO261-183) from another region reported the same error, indicating a global or multi-region manifestation that requires coordinated configuration and testing across environments. Ownership and operational actions were recorded in portal notes (contact persons and consulting company involvement), and the ticket closure explicitly links the TLS/cipher change and server restarts to the remediation outcome. ## TI-722511 is a production-impacting, multi-environment incident resolved by TLS/cipher-suite changes and server restarts The ticket TI-722511 records reproducible errors accessing Financial Reports across UAT and PROD and documents closure text indicating that best-practice cipher suites were activated with IISCRYPTO and AOS servers were restarted as the remediation that resolved the issue. This linkage between the ticket, the tool (IISCRYPTO), the configuration change and the server restarts is explicitly documented in the closure notes and portal history [Data: Entities (0); Entities (18,20); Relationships (9,34,36)]. Because the ticket affects both UAT and PROD and includes symptom reports from EMEA finance users (report creators appearing as "unknown" and inability to open reports), this is a production-grade operational incident rather than a single-user problem; the ticket metadata and relationships show cross-environment impact and operator action items that are relevant to architecture and runbook updates [Data: Entities (0); Relationships (1,10)]. ## AOS and IIS servers are central operational dependencies for the reporting service AOS servers broker requests from user clients (browser/Report Designer) to backend reporting services and were explicitly restarted as part of the remediation, showing they are a required operational touchpoint when TLS/cipher changes are applied. Operationally this means any change to the TLS configuration (cipher suites or minimum TLS versions) or a cumulative OS/security patch may introduce incompatibilities between client/report-designer, IIS, AOS and backend services; the incident remediation (activating best-practice ciphers and restarting servers) and the diagnostic checklist in the records both point to these items as the first-line troubleshooting steps [Data: Entities (0,20,480,488); Relationships (9,803,36)]. ## The incident shows cross-regional recurrence — internal Jira ICO261-183 reports the same error An internal JIRA ticket (ICO261-183) opened by another internal team (Mexico) reported the same error, and the portal notes for TI-722511 reference that JIRA ticket, indicating the problem was observed outside a single operating region and should be treated as a global/tenant-level issue requiring coordinated remediation and communication [Data: Entities (17); Relationships (33); Entities (0); Relationships (0)]. While external support can accelerate remediation, the records show multiple actors (customer contact, consulting company, internal teams, and referenced JIRA) are involved; this multiplicity increases coordination overhead and suggests the need to codify explicit escalation and handoff steps (who is authorized to change TLS settings, who validates post-change testing, and who owns cross-region communication) to reduce confusion during future incidents [Data: Entities (2,24,17); Relationships (1,16,33)]. ## The ticket closure documents a fix but there is limited explicit trace of pre- and post-change testing and rollback procedures Closure text states best-practice cipher suites were activated with IISCRYPTO and servers restarted, and this outcome is associated with resolution of the ticket, but the dataset does not include detailed pre-change compatibility testing results, a staged rollout record, or an explicit rollback plan linked to the change [Data: Entities (0,18,20); Relationships (9,34)]. For production TLS changes that can break client compatibility, the absence of documented staged testing or rollback guidance in the ticket and relationships represents an operational risk — future changes should include mandatory compatibility validation (Report Designer and browser tests across supported clients), versioned configuration snapshots, and an approved rollback procedure recorded in the change ticket before applying IISCRYPTO or similar tooling [Data: Entities (0,480,18); Relationships (9,34,35)]. ## Recommended immediate actions: formalize TLS change control, add AOS/IIS restart playbook, and update monitoring/alerts Given the documented remediation and dependencies, the highest-priority operational actions are: 1) formalize a TLS/cipher-suite change control process that enforces synchronized configuration across UAT and PROD and requires pre-change validation; 2) add a concise runbook for TLS changes that includes steps to use IISCRYPTO, how and when to restart IIS and AOS servers, and post-change verification for report access; and 3) tune monitoring/alerts to detect report designer failures, unknown report-owner symptoms, and TLS negotiation failures so teams can detect regressions quickly. REPORT 25: # NDES / AD CS Certificate Enrollment Community (NDES, Issuing CA, Templates, Devices) This community centers on the Network Device Enrollment Service (NDES) as the operational bridge that automates certificate issuance from Active Directory Certificate Services (AD CS) to target devices (network appliances and managed endpoints). NDES depends directly on AD CS (Issuing CA and certificate templates) and on AD CS post-configuration steps being completed; it also integrates with Intune and targets both network devices and managed endpoints for automated enrollment. The Issuing CA and Certificate Templates are critical configuration points: misconfigured templates or incorrect CA selection commonly cause issuance failures, while Root CA and Issuing CA operational problems (expiry or mismanagement) break certificate validation across consumers. Key operational failure modes called out in the corpus include missing IIS components or NDES service account misconfiguration, incomplete AD CS post-configuration, and template/CA misconfiguration; the decision-tree used for troubleshooting explicitly gates issuance testing on AD CS post-configuration and template correctness. [Data: Entities (360, 364, 365, 370, 369, 375, 376, 363), Relationships (584, 589, 590, 592, 594, 595, 601, 602)] ## NDES is the central operational component connecting AD CS to device consumers NDES functions as the active enrollment service that requests certificates from AD CS and provisions them to devices and managed endpoints; this makes it the focal point for certificate-based device authentication in the environment [Data: Entities (360), Relationships (584, 590, 595)]. Because NDES both initiates requests (to the Issuing CA using configured templates) and delivers certificates to targets (network devices and managed endpoints), failures in NDES manifest as downstream authentication and connectivity problems for many device types—making timely detection and remediation critical for device management and network security posture [Data: Relationships (589, 594, 595)]. ## Common NDES failure modes are documented and actionable The NDES description explicitly lists operational failure modes including inability to start the service, missing IIS components, service-account misconfiguration, AD CS post-configuration errors, and broken communication between NDES and the CA; these are primary troubleshooting paths to investigate when issuance fails [Data: Entities (360)]. The decision-tree logic in the blueprint enforces verification steps (for example, checking AD CS post-configuration) before attempting issuance tests, which both documents these frequent failure causes and provides an operational sequence for root-cause isolation [Data: Entities (360), Relationships (592)]. ## Certificate template misconfiguration is a frequent, high-impact root cause Certificate templates define key usage, validity, and subject fields for issued certificates and are explicitly called out as a common cause of failed automated issuance when misconfigured; the blueprint recommends documentation and regular review of templates to prevent these issues [Data: Entities (365)]. Operationally, templates are consumed by the Issuing CA to produce end-entity certificates; if templates are incorrect on the CA side or selected incorrectly by NDES, issuance will fail or produce certificates incompatible with device expectations, causing authentication errors across the environment [Data: Relationships (602, 589)]. ## CA hierarchy (Root CA → Issuing CA) is a single point of trust and failure The Root CA is the trust anchor that signs the Issuing CA certificate; therefore issues at the Root CA or Issuing CA (expiry, mismanagement, incorrect chaining) can break certificate validation for all issued certificates and impact broad sets of devices and services [Data: Entities (363, 364), Relationships (601)]. Because NDES must be configured to use the correct Issuing CA, any mismatch between NDES configuration and the operational Issuing CA (or problems with the CA chain) will prevent successful enrollment and cause widespread authentication failures until corrected [Data: Relationships (584, 602)]. ## Network devices and managed endpoints are critical targets and verification points The blueprint identifies network appliances (routers, switches, firewalls, VPN gateways) and Intune-managed endpoints as primary recipients of NDES-issued certificates; these device classes should be part of any enrollment verification and post-change validation tests [Data: Entities (375, 376, 370), Relationships (594, 595, 590)]. Because successful issuance is verified by testing enrollment on targeted devices as the final decision-tree step, operational runbooks and monitoring should include sample enrollments against representative device models and managed endpoint profiles to detect regressions early [Data: Relationships (590)]. ## AD CS post-configuration is a gating dependency for NDES operation The corpus explicitly states that AD CS post-configuration (registration of NDES components, enabling required templates, selecting the correct Issuing CA) must be completed before proceeding to issuance tests; the decision tree checks this as a distinct step, making it a documented dependency that must be verified during deployments and troubleshooting [Data: Entities (369), Relationships (592)]. Operational processes should therefore include a checklist for AD CS post-configuration sign-off as a prerequisite for NDES activation and for any change that affects templates or CA selection to avoid wasted troubleshooting effort and false negatives during enrollment testing [Data: Entities (369), Relationships (592)]. ## Recommended operational controls: template governance, CA lifecycle management, and NDES platform hardening The dataset highlights template documentation and regular review as mitigations against issuance failures; implementing template governance (versioning, approvals, change logging) directly addresses a repeatedly-cited root cause [Data: Entities (365)]. Similarly, because Root CA and Issuing CA issues break validation widely, organizations need CA lifecycle controls (expiry monitoring, dedicated owners, offline Root CA handling procedures) to reduce systemic risk; these controls are supported by the Root CA and Issuing CA descriptions that call out expiry and mismanagement as service-impacting events [Data: Entities (363, 364), Relationships (601)]. Finally, hardening and operational checks for NDES—ensuring correct IIS components, validating the NDES service account, and maintaining reliable connectivity between NDES and the Issuing CA—map directly to the NDES failure modes and should be codified in installation and runbook procedures [Data: Entities (360)]. ## Suggested stakeholder/escalation pathway derived from dependency graph The relationship map shows NDES depending on AD CS (Issuing CA and templates) and provisioning certificates to devices, which implies a practical escalation sequence: NDES operations (first responder) → Issuing CA/AD CS administrators (certificate/template investigations) → Root CA owners (if trust-chain/expiry issues) → device owners (network or endpoint teams) for remediation and verification [Data: Entities (360, 364, 363, 370), Relationships (584, 601, 590)]. Making this path explicit in runbooks will shorten mean-time-to-resolution by directing incidents to the correct technical owner quickly—particularly for issues like template misconfiguration or CA chain problems that require CA-side changes rather than device-side fixes [Data: Relationships (589, 602, 590)].

Kind regards Consulting Sample Company REPORT 92: # Zurich: Firewall Outbound UDP Restriction (3478-3481) Impacting Teams Media A location-specific security hardening change deployed at the Zurich site modified outbound firewall rules so that only UDP ports 3478-3481 (STUN/TURN/ICE) were permitted; the dynamic RTP port range (UDP 49152-53247) required for Teams media bypass was blocked, causing media/RTP failure and one-way audio. A temporary test that opened the wider UDP range immediately restored bidirectional audio, confirming the firewall outbound port restriction as the root cause. The incident implicates configuration drift from the Teams media port requirements documented in the blueprint and suggests a change-control / operational gap for location-specific weekend changes. ## Root cause: security-hardening change applied at Zurich created a restrictive outbound firewall rule The incident is directly linked to a security-hardening change applied over the weekend at the Zurich location that modified outbound firewall rules, which introduced a restrictive rule permitting only UDP 3478-3481. The Zurich hardening change is described as the operational change that introduced the restrictive outbound port policy [Data: Entities (452); Entities (442); Relationships (741)]. Because the change was location-specific and applied outside standard business hours (weekend), there is an operational note of a targeted configuration applied that directly resulted in the restrictive rule being present on the site perimeter/firewall. This establishes a clear chain from change to misconfiguration to service impact [Data: Entities (452); Relationships (741)]. ## Configuration mismatch: firewall allowed STUN/TURN but blocked dynamic RTP ports required for Teams media bypass The restrictive firewall rule permitted only the STUN/TURN/ICE ports (UDP 3478-3481) while blocking the dynamic RTP port range (UDP 49152-53247) used by Teams for direct media/SBC connectivity when Media Bypass is used. This mismatch prevented client RTP from reaching the SBC and caused media flow failure and one-way audio symptoms [Data: Entities (442); Entities (443); Relationships (739); Relationships (742)]. The relationships explicitly describe allowance of discovery/ICE signaling while preventing establishment of client RTP media, which explains why signaling might appear to work while audio fails or is one-way when media bypass is active [Data: Relationships (742); Entities (451)]. ## Test validation: temporary opening of dynamic UDP ports immediately restored audio, confirming the firewall as root cause A temporary test that opened a wider UDP port range, including the dynamic RTP ports, immediately restored bidirectional RTP/audio in tests. Reapplying the restrictive rule removed audio again, demonstrating a reproducible cause-and-effect between firewall port filtering and Teams media failure [Data: Entities (448); Relationships (733)]. This test result provides strong evidence for a network-layer blockage rather than client or SBC misconfiguration, and it should be treated as actionable confirmation of the firewall as the root cause [Data: Entities (448); Relationships (733)]. ## Blueprint/documentation alignment: Teams media port requirements exist but were not enforced in firewall configuration The Teams media ports are documented to require UDP 3478-3481 for STUN/TURN/ICE and dynamic RTP ports 49152-53247 for media; the restrictive rule allowed only the STUN/TURN ports and omitted the dynamic RTP range, representing a deviation from the documented requirements in the blueprint [Data: Entities (443); Entities (451); Relationships (743)]. This indicates a gap between the documented network requirements for Teams and the actual implemented firewall policy at the Zurich site; remediation should include a reconciliation of live firewall rules against the blueprint and automated policy checks to prevent divergence [Data: Entities (443); Relationships (743)]. ## Operational/change-management exposure: location-specific weekend change introduced customer-impacting behavior The security-hardening change was location-specific and applied over the weekend at Zurich; that timing and scope contributed to an operational exposure where the side-effect (blocking dynamic RTP ports) impacted Teams media availability at the site [Data: Entities (452); Relationships (741)]. While the data does not enumerate the change approval steps, the fact that a single-site hardening change produced a service outage implies insufficient verification or rollback controls for localized, out-of-hours changes—areas that should be addressed in change management and deployment gating [Data: Entities (452); Relationships (741)]. ## Service impact and recurrence risk: Teams media bypass configurations are fragile without consistent port policies Because Media Bypass depends on direct RTP flows between clients and the SBC using dynamic port ranges, any firewall rule that restricts outbound dynamic RTP ports will reliably cause media failures or one-way audio at affected sites; this creates a systemic recurrence risk if similar hardening actions are applied elsewhere without cross-checking Teams port requirements [Data: Entities (443); Entities (442); Relationships (739)]. The documented restoration by temporarily opening the ports demonstrates both the ease of inducing and resolving this failure mode—meaning other sites or future hardening changes could reproduce the outage unless controls are put in place to validate Teams port requirements before deployment [Data: Entities (448); Relationships (733)]. ## Priority remediation and governance recommendations Short-term remediation confirmed by the data is to restore outbound UDP coverage to include dynamic RTP ports (49152-53247) for affected Zurich perimeter devices; the temporary test that opened these ports immediately restored audio and should be used as the validation step for any permanent rule update [Data: Entities (448); Relationships (733)]. Medium- to long-term recommendations (actionable from the incident evidence) include: 1) reconcile and enforce firewall policies against the Teams media port blueprint across all sites; 2) require pre-deployment validation and automated policy checks for any location-specific hardening change; 3) add a runbook for Media Bypass failures that includes a port-check test (open/close test documented here) and escalation path to network/security owners; and 4) implement monitoring/alerts for denied outbound RTP flows to detect regressions quickly. The need for these steps is grounded in the documented root cause (the hardening change that produced a restrictive firewall rule) and the test evidence that opening the RTP range restores service [Data: Entities (452); Entities (442); Entities (443); Entities (448); Relationships (741); Relationships (733)].

The NETWORK TEAM identified and remediated the issue by aligning the NAT/session timeout with the internal Direct Routing blueprint and adjusting firewall rules; they also coordinated with the MAIL TEAM to restore SMTP relay access for the Basel printer VLAN. Secondary service impacts included disrupted printing delivery (MFP-BASEL-01 intended as the Basel default) and an NDES connector failing to reach Intune due to incomplete outbound firewall rules. The investigation referenced an internal Teams Direct Routing blueprint and resulted in configuration changes that restored service but exposed gaps in change governance for VLAN/NAT policy rollouts and firewall allow-lists. ## Primary root cause: Voice VLAN NAT/UDP session timeout misconfiguration causing VoIP session drops The Basel site implemented a new Voice VLAN that used a different NAT policy and UDP session timeout (30 seconds) than other locations; this short timeout caused SIP/RTP sessions to lose NAT state and led to call disconnects. The relationship between the Voice VLAN and VoIP traffic explicitly links the VLAN's NAT behavior to VoIP session persistence issues, and the NAT/session timeout record documents the 30s setting and the later adjustment to align with the architecture-recommended value. This misconfiguration was diagnosed and corrected by adjusting the NAT/UDP session timeout to match the recommended architecture (for example, 180s), which restored session persistence and call stability. The investigation referenced the Direct Routing/session-handling blueprint as the justification for the new timeout value. [Data: Entities (325, 335, 326); Relationships (532, 536, 537, 520)] ## Operational impact extended beyond voice — affected printing and device defaulting at Basel The Basel location is the physical site of the incident and was intended to use a Basel-local multifunction printer (MFP-BASEL-01) as the default device for users; the site-level network behavior and VLAN changes contributed to delivery/connectivity problems for printing services. The entity for the Basel location describes that the Basel site had the new Voice VLAN and that MFP-BASEL-01 is the intended default printer for Basel users. While voice instability was the primary visible symptom, the same misaligned network policies and omitted allow-list entries impacted SMTP relay and print delivery for devices on the Basel VLAN, demonstrating multi-service impact from network segmentation and policy changes. [Data: Entities (60, 325); Relationships (82, 532)] ## Network Team ownership and coordinated remediation actions were central to recovery The NETWORK TEAM is documented as the responsible group for VLAN, NAT, and firewall configurations; they reviewed the firewall settings, corrected NAT/session timeouts at Basel to match the architecture, and coordinated with the Mail Team to update SMTP relay permissions that had omitted the printer VLAN. The record for the network team captures their role in implementing these changes and performing firewall analysis and troubleshooting. Their actions included both the NAT/session timeout adjustment (aligned to the Direct Routing blueprint) and firewall rule modifications to restore connectivity for services such as the NDES connector and the print server, indicating effective cross-team coordination during remediation. [Data: Entities (141); Relationships (520, 1118, 224)] ## Firewall/NSG rules caused or prolonged outages for NDES/Intune and other services Firewall rules (perimeter, host-level, and Azure NSGs) were identified as a recurring root cause for services reporting an unhealthy state; in this case, incomplete firewall rules blocked the NDES connector from communicating with Intune and prevented required outbound HTTPS/DNS traffic. The FIREWALL RULES entity documents that these controls must permit outbound HTTPS (TCP 443), DNS, and internal AD ports, and that adjustments by the Network Team restored connectivity for the NDES connector. This demonstrates that beyond NAT timeouts, explicit firewall allow-lists and NSG configurations are critical for device management and cloud-service integrations; missing entries (for printer VLANs or connectors) will cause service degradation even when routing and session persistence are correct. [Data: Entities (643, 141); Relationships (1118)] ## Blueprint (Direct Routing & session handling) was authoritative and used to justify remediation, highlighting governance gaps The internal blueprint for Microsoft Teams Direct Routing & session handling was referenced during the investigation and used to guide the final NAT/session timeout adjustment at Basel, indicating available architectural guidance for voice session handling. The ticket explicitly cites the blueprint as the basis for aligning the Basel configuration to the recommended session timers. However, the fact that the Basel Voice VLAN deviated from the blueprint (using a 30s timeout) indicates a governance or change-control gap when introducing new VLANs or NAT policies; the deviation required detection and correction rather than being prevented at rollout. [Data: Entities (328, 326); Relationships (520, 537)] ## SMTP relay omission impacted printer external delivery and required Mail Team coordination Investigation showed the printer VLAN had been omitted from the SMTP relay allow list, which affected external delivery for devices on that VLAN. The NETWORK TEAM coordinated with the MAIL TEAM to confirm the omission and implement relay configuration changes that restored external mail delivery for the printer. This is a clear example of how network segmentation and mail relay policies must be coordinated during network changes; failure to include new VLANs in allow-lists manifests as service outages for devices that rely on SMTP relay. [Data: Relationships (224); Entities (141, 60, 325)] ## Residual risk: recurrence likely without standardized NAT/firewall change controls and validation Although remediation restored voice and device connectivity, the underlying causes (nonstandard NAT timeout on a voice VLAN and omitted firewall/mail allow-lists) are change-control and configuration-management issues that can recur when new VLANs or policies are deployed without standardized templates, automated validation, or checklist-driven reviews. The Basel case links the new Voice VLAN and its differing NAT policy to service failure, and the firewall/SMTP omissions required reactive fixes. To reduce recurrence, controls should codify blueprint-recommended NAT/session timers, require pre-deployment validation that VLANs are included in necessary allow-lists (SMTP, management/Intune, AD), and add monitoring/alerts for abnormal NAT session churn or call-quality metrics. These recommendations are grounded in the observed entities and relationships tying the Voice VLAN, NAT policy, firewall rules, and blueprint to the incident. Kind regards Consulting Sample Company REPORT 74: # SBC Translation Table / Direct Routing — Missing New Number Range This community centers on the PSTN-to-Teams Direct Routing chain: the Carrier/Provider delivers calls to the customer's SBC, the SBC translation table implements the Routing and Translation Rules (including E.164 normalization), and those rules feed Voice Routing Policies that route calls to Teams. During a recent migration the carrier provisioned a New Number Range which was partially migrated by the customer; those new numbers (+41 44 785 23 10/11 are given as examples) were not present in the SBC translation table and associated routing rules, causing INVITEs for that range to be mis-answered (404) and not forwarded to Teams. The carrier confirmed upstream delivery to the SBC, helping isolate the failure to SBC-side translation/routing configuration rather than upstream provisioning. [Data: Entities (177,178,179,180,181,183), Relationships (274,276,278,279,282,275)] ## Primary technical root cause: missing new-range entries in the SBC translation table Investigation identified that new telephone numbers provisioned by the carrier were not present in the SBC translation table, so INVITEs for that range were mis-answered (404) and not forwarded into Teams. This indicates a configuration gap where the concrete SBC translation entries did not include the new number range(s) introduced during migration [Data: Entities (178,183), Relationships (276,282)]. Because the SBC translation table is the concrete implementation of the Routing and Translation Rules, an omission at that configuration artifact directly prevented normalization and mapping for the affected numbers, breaking the call delivery chain to Teams until the entries were added to the table. This is consistent with the relationships tying the translation table to routing rules and the old/new range contrast observed during troubleshooting [Data: Entities (178,179,183), Relationships (276,282)]. ## Carrier confirmation isolates failure to SBC-side processing (not upstream delivery) The carrier/provider reported that they delivered calls correctly to the SBC and saw no routing errors on their side, which helped rule out upstream delivery issues and focus the investigation on SBC processing and configuration [Data: Entities (177), Relationships (274)]. Operationally, this eliminates carrier routing as the immediate cause and shortens the troubleshooting path to SBC translation and normalization logic, which should be the primary focus for immediate remediation and validation steps. The existing carrier confirmation should be recorded in the incident timeline and retained as evidence for post-incident review and change verification [Data: Relationships (274)]. ## Dependency chain: Routing & Translation Rules → E.164 normalization → Voice Routing Policies The components form a linear dependency chain where Routing and Translation Rules implement E.164 normalization and feed normalized numbers into Voice Routing Policies that determine Teams delivery; failures within the translation layer therefore propagate downstream to voice routing and call delivery [Data: Relationships (278,279), Entities (179,181,180)]. This chain highlights a single-point-of-impact: while multiple components participate in call delivery, the translation table acts as the authoritative mapping layer. Any gaps or misconfigurations in the translation table or the translation rules will prevent normalized address generation and thus prevent voice routing policies from matching and delivering calls to Teams users or voicemail/auto-attendants [Data: Entities (178,179,180)]. ## Migration and change-control gap: new range was provisioned but translation rules were not updated The new number range was assigned/provisioned by the carrier and partially migrated by the customer the week prior to the issue, creating a requirement to update SBC translation rules that was not met prior to cutover; the relationship metadata explicitly links the migration to the need to update SBC entries [Data: Entities (183), Relationships (275)]. This sequence suggests a process gap between telecommunications provisioning and SBC configuration change (e.g., missing checklist item, absent automated provisioning, or an incomplete change request). That control gap materially increased the likelihood of service disruption for the new range and should be addressed by process and governance changes (see recommendations). ## Observed failure mode: INVITEs mis-answered with 404 and not forwarded to Teams The direct symptom reported was that INVITEs for numbers in the new range were mis-answered with 404 responses and were not forwarded into Teams, consistent with missing or mismatched normalization/mapping entries on the SBC that prevented routing policies from matching the calls to internal destinations [Data: Entities (178,183), Relationships (276,282)]. This failure mode is reproducible and actionable: adding the correct normalization and translation entries for the new range should return correct call handling and prevent 404 mis-answers for those numbers. As such, verification steps can be scripted into a runbook for validation after updates are applied. ## Immediate remediation priorities and validation steps The highest-priority corrective action is to add the new-number-range entries to the SBC translation table and ensure the corresponding routing/translation rules implement correct E.164 normalization so the Voice Routing Policies match the calls to Teams endpoints [Data: Entities (178,179,181), Relationships (276,279,278)]. Follow-up validation should include (a) inbound test calls from the carrier for affected sample numbers, (b) SBC logs verifying correct translation and normalization, (c) verification that calls reach Teams and that no 404s are returned, and (d) a change-control record that ties the carrier provisioning event to the SBC configuration change to close the loop. Retain the carrier’s confirmation of upstream delivery as supporting evidence [Data: Entities (177,183), Relationships (274,275)]. ## Operational risk and recurrence: process and monitoring deficiencies The root cause and migration timeline point to a repeatable operational risk: when carriers provision new ranges, failure to propagate those ranges into SBC translation rules will repeatedly cause call delivery failures. The presence of an existing correctly functioning old number range makes such omissions less obvious until customers or users on the new range report failures [Data: Entities (184,183), Relationships (282)]. Mitigations should include: a) formalized handoff checklist between carrier provisioning and SBC configuration owners, b) automated reconciliation that compares carrier-provided ranges to SBC translation table contents, c) monitoring/alerting for abnormal 404 rates on the SBC for inbound PSTN INVITEs, and d) test call validation as part of cutover acceptance. ## Stakeholder mapping and escalation pathway Key stakeholders in this community are the Carrier/Provider (responsible for PSTN provisioning and initial delivery), the SBC owner/telephony/networking team (responsible for translation tables and routing rules), and the Teams/UC platform team (consumer of normalized addresses via Voice Routing Policies). The relationships show direct handoffs from Carrier → SBC and SBC configuration → Voice Routing Policies that affect Teams delivery [Data: Entities (177,178,180,179), Relationships (274,278)]. Escalation should follow: (1) carrier confirms delivery (already done) to quickly isolate the issue, (2) SBC/telephony team applies translation updates and notifies Teams/UC team, (3) Teams/UC validates call delivery and closes the loop, and (4) change-control and post-incident review document the migration and corrective steps for governance and audit. context: SOURCE 8: ﻿ Ticket summary: Ticket name: KF52: Microsoft Teams – Incoming calls from specific numbers not received Ticket number: TI-1612036 Company: Customer Company 52 AG Contact person: Contact Person 22 Owner: Employee 41 Origin: Email Type: Incident Created on: 18.09.25 08:56 Closure Closure date: 29.09.25 16:18 Closure text: Analysis completed. Routing and translation rules adjusted according to Direct Routing Blueprint. Incoming calls fully functional again. Activities 18.09.25 08:40 – Email from Contact Person 22 Good morning We have noticed that incoming calls from certain customers are no longer reaching us. Callers report that they either receive a busy tone or the connection is disconnected after a few seconds. Other external calls are functioning normally. Could you please check this? Kind regards 18.09.25 08:56 Ticket created and assigned to Employee 41. 18.09.25 09:25 – Helpdesk inquiry Good day Could you please provide the following information: – Example phone numbers where the issue occurs – Since when the issue has existed – Whether all users are affected or only specific ones Thank you. 18.09.25 10:15 – Customer response Here are two examples: +41 44 785 23 10 +41 44 785 23 11 Other numbers such as +41 31 … work without issue. The problem has existed since last week. All employees are affected. 18.09.25 11:40 – Internal analysis Test call from private mobile (different number range) → works. Test with number in specified range → call not delivered. In the Teams Admin Center, no incoming call is shown. 19.09.25 09:05 – Initial hypothesis Possible provider issue. Carrier contacted. 19.09.25 14:20 – Carrier feedback From the carrier’s perspective, the call is correctly delivered to the company’s SBC. No routing errors visible on their side. 22.09.25 08:55 – SBC log analysis For functioning numbers: INVITE accepted and forwarded to Teams. For affected numbers: INVITE received but immediately answered with “404 Not Found”. 22.09.25 11:30 – Email to customer Good day We see that incoming calls from the provider are reaching the SBC. However, the requests are not being correctly forwarded internally. Were there recent changes to number ranges or new extensions? Kind regards 22.09.25 13:05 – Customer response Yes, we received a new number range from the provider last week and partially migrated old numbers. The affected numbers belong to this new range. 23.09.25 09:10 – Internal review Comparison with document “Blueprint – Microsoft Teams Direct Routing & Number Translation – Version 2.3” Section regarding: • Normalization of incoming numbers • E.164 format • Translation rules on SBC • Voice routing policies Finding: New numbers are not included in the SBC translation table. Routing rule covers only the old number range. 24.09.25 – Test Temporary adjustment of translation rule for new number range. Test call performed. Call correctly signaled and delivered. Reverting to original configuration → issue reproducible. 25.09.25 – Coordination with customer Confirmation that new number range was fully migrated. Internal documentation had not yet been updated. 26.09.25 – Adjustment Routing and translation rules extended according to architectural specification. Details not documented in ticket for security reasons. 29.09.25 – Customer feedback All tested numbers are working again. Multiple customers also confirmed successful calls. Thank you for the support. 29.09.25 16:18 – Ticket closure Dear Contact Person 22 The root cause was an inconsistent translation and routing configuration following the introduction of a new number range. The configuration was adjusted according to the internal Direct Routing architecture. Incoming calls are now correctly delivered again. Kind regards Consulting Sample Company

Kind regards 06.11.25 08:37 Ticket created and assigned to Employee 21. 06.11.25 09:10 – Initial analysis Remote access to an affected user. Print job briefly appears in the print server queue, then disappears without printing. Queue itself is not paused. Print Spooler service is running. Test print directly from the print server to the printer also fails. 06.11.25 10:05 – Inquiry to customer Good day Were there any recent changes to the network or infrastructure at the Basel location? Thank you for your brief feedback. 06.11.25 11:20 – Customer response Last week, a new network segment for devices was introduced. The printers were moved into a separate VLAN. No other changes are known to us. 07.11.25 09:00 – Further analysis Ping from print server to printer unsuccessful. Ping from a client within the same location network successful. This indicates segmentation between the server network and the printer VLAN. Comparison with document “Print & Scanning Infrastructure Standard – Version 1.0”. In the section Location Concept, it is defined that printers operate in a separate VLAN but must be reachable from the print server. Routing and firewall rules must be configured accordingly. 07.11.25 11:30 – Internal coordination with Network Team Confirmation that the new firewall rule did not allow the server network access to the printer VLAN. Rule adjusted according to standard. 10.11.25 – Test phase Printing from print server successful. Multiple test prints from user workstations performed. No abnormalities observed. 12.11.25 14:40 – Customer feedback Printer is functioning normally again. Larger print jobs are also processed correctly. 12.11.25 14:52 – Ticket closure Dear Contact Person 15 The root cause was a missing network allowance between the print server and the newly configured printer VLAN at the Basel location. The configuration was adjusted according to the “Print & Scanning Infrastructure Standard”. The printer is fully reachable again. All other employees in the same room can print without issue. The affected user receives the message “Access denied” when attempting to print. 02.12.25 09:03 Ticket created and assigned to Employee 27. 02.12.25 09:20 – Initial analysis Remote connection established with the affected user. The permission structure was cleaned up according to the “Print & Scanning Infrastructure Standard”. Printing is now fully functional again. Kind regards Consulting Sample Company REPORT 41: # Customer Company 52 AG — Microsoft Teams Direct Routing & Endpoint Incidents (Zurich/Basel) The community centers on CUSTOMER COMPANY 52 AG's Microsoft Teams environment (KF52) and a small cluster of user‑facing incidents affecting audio, camera devices, and PSTN routing after a carrier/number migration. Key operational actors are Employee 33 (first‑line support), System Engineer 18 and BU Unified Communications (specialist Direct Routing/VoIP), and named customer contacts (Contact Person 14 and Contact Person 09). The dominant operational failure was a media‑flow restriction caused by a recent firewall configuration change at the Zurich site that produced one‑way audio for roughly a dozen users; this was diagnosed by System Engineer 18 and resolved by restoring configuration to the Direct Routing architecture and validating stability with a 48‑hour monitoring window. Other separate issues included a camera not detected case resolved by applying device permission steps from the Teams client blueprint and multiple headset reports that indicated the symptom was not isolated to a single hardware vendor. An internal documentation gap (outdated numbering/routing docs) contributed to an SBC translation table oversight during a number migration, increasing the risk of missed incoming calls across the tenant. Communication and diagnostics used email and remote sessions, and remediation involved coordination across support, networking/firewall, and unified communications teams. ## Primary outage cause: firewall media restriction at Zurich broke Teams Direct Routing media flow The one‑way audio incident documented in ticket TI‑1602241 was diagnosed as a media‑flow restriction introduced by a recent firewall change at the Zurich location; System Engineer 18 analyzed SBC and firewall logs, coordinated the configuration change per the Direct Routing architecture, and validated restored voice transmission before closing the ticket. This indicates the outage was infrastructure (network/firewall) induced rather than an application bug or client misconfiguration, and it required cross‑team coordination to remediate. [Data: Entities (107); Relationships (721,162,717,+more)] ## Clear escalation and ownership path exists but spans multiple teams Operational handling followed a common support escalation chain: Employee 33 performed initial triage and hand‑offs, escalating to BU Unified Communications for specialist Direct Routing/media‑flow work, where System Engineer 18 performed in‑depth analysis and implemented changes. That hand‑over model—first‑line support -> specialist UC team -> network/firewall coordination—worked to resolve the incident but also implies dependency on specialist availability for media‑path issues. Documented interactions show Employee 33 as ticket owner and initial troubleshooter, System Engineer 18 taking advanced troubleshooting, and the ticket being forwarded to BU Unified Communications for VoIP expertise. Separately, a distinct camera not detected ticket (TI‑1615042) was resolved by applying steps from the Teams client devices & permissions blueprint (OS privacy and app permissions), demonstrating that endpoint issues still occur but are often remedied by following existing device/permission runbooks. These two vectors illustrate the need to differentiate symptomatic device failures from systemic network faults during initial triage. [Data: Entities (123,446,735,737); Relationships (170,718,1317,1323)] ## Documentation and change‑control gap increased risk during carrier/number migration Customer internal operational documentation describing numbering and routing had not been updated following a number migration; this documentation gap contributed to an SBC translation table not being updated in time and to subsequent missed incoming calls across the company. The presence of an outdated internal doc tied to the tenant (separate from the official Direct Routing blueprint) indicates a governance and configuration‑management weakness that can translate directly into service impact during migrations. Addressing documentation currency and change verification would reduce similar risks. [Data: Entities (187,108); Relationships (265)] ## Remediation included architecture‑aligned configuration change plus monitoring to validate stability After the firewall rule/configuration was corrected to conform with the Direct Routing architecture, voice transmission was validated as stable and a 48‑hour monitoring period recorded no anomalies before ticket closure. Remote sessions enabled direct verification of audio levels and device connections and were used to remediate client/endpoint issues (camera and physical headset reconnection). This combination of channels supports both initial reporting and deep troubleshooting but requires clear logging and handover notes to preserve context across teams. [Data: Entities (673,740,736); Relationships (1320,1178)] ## Operational scope: localized but multi‑site impacts and multiple related tickets indicate elevated operational risk While the primary media restriction incident impacted roughly a dozen users at Zurich, the tenant experienced multiple related incidents (other tickets referenced for KF52) and reported missed incoming calls across the company after a carrier migration, affecting locations including Zurich and Basel and a small Sales group. However, an outdated internal routing document (separate from the official blueprint) undermined operational execution during number migration.
