REPORT 100: # SAP Print Servers — VRINFRGERPRNT03 / VRINFRGERPRNT04 Incident Community This community centers on the SAP print infrastructure (logical group: SAP PRINT SERVERS) and two dedicated Windows VM print servers, VRINFRGERPRNT03 and VRINFRGERPRNT04, which failed to process SAP print jobs during a post‑maintenance window. Operators attempted service-level remediation (Windows Print Spooler restart) and then a VM rollback to a pre‑maintenance snapshot; printing remained broken and both VMs experienced blue screens (BSOD) during post‑restore validation. The incident activated the Infrastructure On‑Call team and involved hands‑on restoration actions with named restore contexts (INTER12203 / INTER12204) and an individual identified as Mister X. A suspected driver/OS/Windows update regression is explicitly called out by stakeholders as a likely root cause. Immediate operational impact is confined to SAP printing on the affected servers but the failure mode—OS instability after rollback—indicates deeper platform or configuration risk that requires coordinated diagnostics, controlled validation, and governance changes to prevent recurrence. [Data: Entities (545, 154, 155); Relationships (913, 907, 914, 908, 911 +more)] ## SAP print cluster and two VM members are the operational center of the incident The logical service affected is the SAP print infrastructure; VRINFRGERPRNT03 and VRINFRGERPRNT04 are explicitly identified as members of the SAP PRINT SERVERS group and were implicated in the failure to deliver SAP print jobs. These two VMs are the primary production endpoints for SAP-generated print output and therefore central to business printing continuity. [Data: Entities (545, 154, 155); Relationships (913, 907, 914)] Because both VMs exhibited the same failure pattern (printing stopped, spooler restarts attempted, then rollback and subsequent crashes), the failure is likely systemic to how these servers are configured or to recent platform changes rather than an isolated queue or application issue. This increases the urgency for cross‑layer diagnostics (OS, drivers, hypervisor/host, and SAP print subsystem). [Data: Entities (154, 155); Relationships (907, 914)] ## Persistent symptom pattern: spooler restart and VM restart did not remediate; BSODs occurred after snapshot rollback Operators performed typical first‑line remediation—restarting the Windows Print Spooler and rebooting the VMs—but these actions did not restore SAP printing functionality. The print spooler restart is recorded as an action (Employee 23) and is associated with the affected servers. [Data: Entities (536); Relationships (921, 907, 914)] Following unsuccessful service restarts, a rollback to a pre‑maintenance snapshot was executed; during subsequent validation both VMs experienced blue screens (BSOD), indicating kernel/driver/OS instability rather than a transient spooler process fault. The appearance of BSODs after a restore suggests the underlying fault either persisted across snapshots or was triggered by the restoration sequence, requiring deeper forensic analysis (memory dumps, event logs, driver lists, and host/hypervisor checks). [Data: Entities (542, 154, 155); Relationships (911, 908)] ## Windows update / OS version is the suspected root cause and rollback was requested Stakeholders explicitly linked the incident to Windows updates or the OS version and requested a rollback of the Windows version on the print servers, indicating a plausible regression introduced by a recent update. This suspicion is recorded as a request by Contact Person 11 to roll back Windows versioning on the print servers. [Data: Entities (537); Relationships (923)] While a Windows update is a credible lead, the fact that BSODs appeared after a snapshot rollback implies either a driver incompatibility carried in the snapshot or a host/VM mismatch with updated components; the hypothesis should be validated with patch/version inventories, update timelines, and comparison of pre/post maintenance driver and system component lists. [Data: Entities (154, 155); Relationships (908, 923)] ## Recovery actions and actors: Infrastructure On‑Call and named restore contexts were engaged The incident was escalated to the Infrastructure On‑Call team, which authorized and coordinated a rollback to a pre‑maintenance snapshot (17.07 21:00) as a high‑priority recovery action. Hands‑on restoration was performed in the context of identifiers INTER12203 and INTER12204 and involved a technician referenced as Mister X. These actors and contexts should be captured in an incident timeline and runbook for future reproducibility and accountability. [Data: Entities (538, 539, 540, 541); Relationships (908, 924, 925)] Recording the specific restore hosts/contexts and the technicians involved is valuable for post‑incident review: it enables tracing of the precise restore procedure, the source snapshot used, and any host‑level conditions that may have influenced the outcome (for example, host patch level, VM driver compatibility, or snapshot consistency). [Data: Relationships (908, 924, 925)] ## Operational validation and change control gaps evidenced by BSODs after restore The occurrence of blue screens during validation after a rollback highlights insufficient controlled post‑restore validation and possibly gaps in the change control process for Windows updates or VM snapshots. The documented restore to pre‑maintenance and the subsequent BSODs on at least one server are recorded in the incident logs. [Data: Relationships (908, 911); Entities (154, 155)] This pattern suggests the need to strengthen pre/post maintenance validation checklists (including driver signature/compatibility checks, automated health checks, and staged rollouts) and to mandate capture of crash dumps and hypervisor logs immediately after any restore that precedes system instability. Procedural improvements should be tied to the Infrastructure On‑Call workflow so restores are executed under a standardized validation plan. [Data: Entities (538); Relationships (908, 911)] ## Stakeholder communication and ownership are partially recorded but could be clearer There is evidence of collaborative work and internal acknowledgements—Employee 19 took over analysis of BSOD and printing issues and sent internal recognition for contributors—indicating some ownership and handoff actions during the incident. However, responsibility for update rollbacks, forensic analysis, and post‑restore validation is not fully codified in the recorded artifacts. [Data: Entities (516); Relationships (879)] Additionally, an explicit recorded action (Employee 23 restarting the print spooler) shows first‑line troubleshooting occurred, but the logs do not clearly document definitive assignment for root‑cause remediation (who will pursue driver rollbacks, who will gather crash dumps, who will coordinate with application owners). Formalizing owner roles (service owner, platform owner, on‑call lead) in the runbook would reduce ambiguity in future incidents. [Data: Entities (536); Relationships (921, 879)] ## Prioritized, actionable recommendations to reduce recurrence and speed remediation 1) Immediate forensic tasks: collect kernel crash dumps (BSOD) and system event logs from VRINFRGERPRNT03 and VRINFRGERPRNT04, and archive the exact snapshot used for rollback for controlled replay; this is supported by the occurrence of BSODs after restore and the recorded restore action. [Data: Entities (154, 155, 542); Relationships (908, 911)] 2) Validate Windows update hypothesis: perform a controlled rollback of the Windows update/version on a non‑production clone and compare behavior, and inventory recent patches applied to the print servers as requested by Contact Person 11. [Data: Entities (537); Relationships (923)] 3) Strengthen post‑maintenance validation and change control: implement a mandatory post‑restore validation checklist (including driver compatibility checks and health probes) and require Infrastructure On‑Call sign‑off before marking print services as recovered. The restore and on‑call activation events provide the operational trigger points for this control. [Data: Entities (538); Relationships (908)] 4) Update runbooks & escalation: codify ownership for print service incidents (service owner, platform owner, on‑call lead), document restore contexts (INTER12203 / INTER12204) and performer (Mister X) for auditability, and add an escalation ladder for failures that persist after service restart. Existing records of restore contexts and actors should be used to populate runbook templates. [Data: Entities (539, 540, 541); Relationships (924, 925)] 5) Staged rollout and driver management: before broad Windows updates on print VMs, test updates against a staging snapshot of the SAP print servers and maintain a validated list of approved print drivers; given that spooler restarts did not resolve the issue and BSODs followed, driver/OS compatibility is a high‑priority risk to mitigate. [Data: Entities (536, 542, 154); Relationships (907, 911)] REPORT 173: # SAP Print Servers — VRINFRGERPRNT03/04 Windows‑Update Incident Two dedicated SAP print‑server VMs (VRINFRGERPRNT03 and VRINFRGERPRNT04) failed to print SAP jobs after a maintenance window. Initial remediation focused on restarting the Windows Print Spooler and server reboots, which did not restore functionality. A rollback to a pre‑maintenance snapshot was performed, after which both servers exhibited blue screens (BSOD). The evidence in the corpus points to a Windows update or OS/version change as the suspected root cause; the infrastructure on‑call team was activated and a service manager/engineer (Employee 19) took over deeper log analysis and coordination. The failure pattern is consistent across multiple SAP printserver instances, indicating a systemic issue that requires controlled rollback validation, deeper OS/driver/host diagnostics, and tighter change gating and post‑change validation for print infrastructure. ## VRINFRGERPRNT03 and VRINFRGERPRNT04 are the primary affected nodes in a clustered SAP printserver deployment Two specific Windows print‑server VMs (VRINFRGERPRNT03 and VRINFRGERPRNT04) are explicitly identified as SAP print servers implicated in the incident; both host SAP print queues and are members of the SAP PRINT SERVERS logical group. This anchors the outage to a defined, business‑critical service boundary (SAP printing) rather than an isolated workstation or single ephemeral VM [Data: Entities (154,155,545); Relationships (913,914,907)]. Because both VMs belong to the same logical SAP printserver group and showed the same failure pattern, remediation and future controls should be designed and tested at the service‑group level (SAP PRINT SERVERS) rather than treating each VM as an entirely independent asset. The records show the same troubleshooting steps and outcomes applied to both hosts, which supports a service‑level view of risk and recovery planning [Data: Entities (154,155,545); Relationships (913,914,907)]. ## Print spooler restarts were attempted but did not remediate the outage The Windows PRINT SPOOLER service was explicitly restarted on the affected machines as an initial remediation action, but this did not restore SAP printing functionality, indicating the root cause is likely below or outside of simple spooler process failure (for example OS, drivers, or a Windows update interaction) [Data: Entities (536); Relationships (907,914,922)]. The operational implication is that runbooks that treat spooler restarts as a primary or sufficient first‑line fix for SAP printserver faults are incomplete for this failure class. Playbooks should capture escalation criteria when spooler restarts fail (for example: collect event logs, suspend further changes, escalate to infrastructure on‑call) to avoid wasted cycles and to accelerate diagnostics [Data: Entities (536); Relationships (907,914,922)]. ## A Windows update / OS version change is the leading suspected root cause and rollback was requested The corpus records an explicit suspicion that a Windows update or Windows version change impacted print functionality and documents a request to roll back the Windows version on affected print servers; the infrastructure on‑call also documented suspicion that a Windows update affected printing, and restores to a pre‑maintenance snapshot were performed as a remedial action [Data: Entities (537); Relationships (910)]. This attribution to Windows updates changes the investigative focus toward update/patch levels, driver compatibility, and recent kernel or print‑related fixes. It also creates an immediate governance action: pauses or holds on further OS updates for the SAP printserver cohort until a controlled validation and remediation plan is established and tested [Data: Entities (537); Relationships (910)]. ## Restores produced blue screens (BSOD) indicating OS/driver/host‑level instability beyond simple spooler faults After the rollback/restore to the pre‑maintenance snapshot, affected servers experienced blue screens (BSOD), which were reported by contact personnel. The presence of BSODs after restore shows the fault is not limited to a transient spooler state and suggests kernel‑level, driver, or VM host/compatibility issues requiring deeper forensic diagnostics (memory dumps, driver lists, host hypervisor logs) [Data: Entities (542,154,155); Relationships (911)]. BSODs materially increase incident severity because they obstruct in‑place troubleshooting and imply potential corruption or incompatibility at the OS or hypervisor layer. Recovery will likely require coordinated work across server OS, virtualization/host, and driver teams and careful post‑restore validation in a controlled environment before returning to production [Data: Entities (542,154,155); Relationships (911)]. ## Infrastructure on‑call was activated and a Service Manager performed deeper analysis and cross‑team coordination The infrastructure ON‑CALL team was specifically activated to restore VRINFRGERPRNT03 to its pre‑maintenance state and coordinate recovery actions, indicating recognized operational escalation to a higher response tier [Data: Entities (538); Relationships (908)]. Additionally, EMPLOYEE 19, a Service Manager who also conducted hands‑on engineering work and further troubleshooting using event/log data, was asked to take over analysis of the BSOD and printing issues and communicated gratitude/recognition internally. This documents clear human ownership for post‑restore analysis and provides a named contact for follow‑up, lessons learned, and runbook updates [Data: Entities (516); Relationships (879,908)]. ## Identical failure pattern across multiple SAP printserver VMs indicates systemic risk and recurrence potential Both VRINFRGERPRNT03 and VRINFRGERPRNT04 exhibited the same failure progression: print failure → spooler/service restarts → rollback to snapshot → blue screens during post‑restore testing. The repeated pattern across multiple hosts strongly suggests a systemic trigger (for example a pushed OS update, shared driver package, or hypervisor change) rather than random independent hardware faults [Data: Entities (154,155); Relationships (914,911,910)]. Systemic faults require group‑level mitigation: suspend further automated or bulk updates for the SAP printserver cohort, perform canary validation of updates on an isolated test instance, and update change controls and testing gates to include print‑job functional tests for SAP output as part of the post‑patch validation checklist [Data: Entities (154,155,537); Relationships (910,914)]. ## Operational controls and runbook gaps exposed: need for controlled post‑change validation, snapshot testing, and escalation criteria The incident shows that a snapshot rollback was used as a recovery step but produced BSODs in testing — this underscores the need for controlled, documented post‑change validation and restore verification processes that include OS/hypervisor compatibility checks and application (SAP) print‑flow tests before reintroducing servers to production [Data: Entities (154,155,542); Relationships (908,911)]. Recommended runbook elements (grounded in the observed actions) include: a) explicit escalation triggers when spooler restarts fail, b) mandatory collection of system event logs and memory dumps prior to rolling back, c) validating snapshots in an isolated host/test environment before applying them to production VMs, and d) a temporary freeze on related Windows updates until a remediation path is confirmed. These steps align with the documented sequence of failed remediations and observed outcomes and reduce the chance of reintroducing unstable server states [Data: Entities (536,542,537); Relationships (907,911,910)]. ## Prioritized actionable recommendations for immediate remediation and medium‑term mitigation Immediate (high priority): suspend further Windows updates or automated OS version changes for the SAP printserver group; quarantine one server for forensic work (collect memory dumps, event logs, driver lists); and perform kernel/driver analysis on collected artifacts. These actions map to the documented suspicion of a Windows update, the failed spooler restarts, and the after‑restore BSODs [Data: Entities (537,536,542); Relationships (907,910,911)]. Near‑term (days–weeks): implement a canary/test instance and require a post‑change functional test (SAP print job round‑trip) before approving updates for the SAP printserver cohort; update change management to require infrastructure on‑call notification and runbook steps when printserver updates are scheduled; and produce a dedicated incident RCA and remediation playbook authored by the infrastructure on‑call and Service Manager (Employee 19) to capture lessons learned and update runbooks [Data: Entities (538,516,537); Relationships (908,879,910)]. Medium‑term (weeks–months): review driver/package versioning and coordination with virtualization/host teams to rule out host compatibility issues, add monitoring for spooler process failures combined with OS‑level health checks, and institutionalize snapshot validation and rollback procedures that include pre‑approval and test validation before rejoining production. These measures address the multi‑layer failure signals captured in the incident records and will reduce recurrence risk across the SAP printserver service group [Data: Entities (154,155,545,542); Relationships (913,911)]. REPORT 102: # SAP Printing Outage — VRINFRGERPRNT03/04 Network-Share Failure This community centers on SAP-originated printing that relied on two named print-server VMs (VRINFRGERPRNT03 and VRINFRGERPRNT04). A Windows update followed by a VM restore sequence is implicated in causing the print servers' SMB/file-share presentation of printer queues to malfunction, which blocked SAP printing until administrators disabled and re-enabled the affected network shares and printer queues to clear pending jobs and restore service. Normal/non-SAP printing continued via a separate follow-me printing path on other print servers, indicating the outage was scoped to the SAP print-path servers. Employee 20 participated in troubleshooting to determine whether the issue was on the SAP side. [Data: Entities (521,522,527,523,524); Relationships (883,887,890,891,892)] ## Primary failure mode: print-server SMB/network share malfunction after Windows update and VM restore The incident root sequence identifies a Windows update followed by a virtual-machine restore as part of the causal chain that led to network-share problems on the print-server VMs [Data: Relationships (890); Entities (523)]. The named print server VRINFRGERPRNT03 is explicitly recorded as having its printer-queue network share malfunction after the update/restore, and administrators cleared the problem by disabling and re-enabling the share on affected queues; VRINFRGERPRNT04 is also listed as unreachable for SAP printing during the incident [Data: Relationships (883,887); Entities (521,522)]. ## Business impact: SAP printing blocked until shares/queues were restored SAP is the originating application for the affected jobs and printing from SAP was blocked while the print-server shares/queues were malfunctioning, indicating direct business-process impact to SAP print-dependent workflows [Data: Entities (527); Relationships (883,887)]. Pending documents accumulated on the configured printer queues and were held until administrators disabled and re-enabled the network share for those queues, after which printing resumed—showing the outage directly caused job backlog and delivery delay for SAP print output [Data: Entities (525); Relationships (891)]. ## Scope was limited to SAP path print servers; follow-me printing and other servers unaffected The incident affected the print servers in the SAP print path (VRINFRGERPRNT03/04) while a separate follow-me printing solution continued to provide normal non-SAP printing via other print servers, indicating the outage was scoped rather than tenant-wide printing disruption [Data: Entities (526,528); Relationships (892); Entities (521,522)]. This isolation suggests different print infrastructures (SAP-targeted VMs vs. follow-me servers) and/or differing configuration/state that made only the SAP print servers susceptible to the update/restore side-effect [Data: Entities (532)]. ## Operational remediation steps were manual and effective but ad-hoc Administrators resolved the immediate blockage by disabling and re-enabling the network share exposing printer queues, which cleared pending documents and allowed printing to resume—this established an effective remediation but is a manual, reactive action tied to operator intervention [Data: Relationships (891); Entities (521)]. Employee 20 from the support team was engaged to investigate whether the cause originated on the SAP side (examining SAP-to-printer interactions), showing cross-team troubleshooting activity during the incident and that ownership boundaries were exercised in the response [Data: Entities (517,527); Relationships (906,879)]. ## Configuration and exposure: printer network share (SMB) is a key dependency and potential risk point The printer network share is specifically described as the SMB/file-share exposure used to present printer queues to clients and SAP; the share's malfunction was the immediate failure surface that blocked jobs, highlighting the share as a critical dependency in the SAP print flow [Data: Entities (524); Relationships (883,891)]. Because SMB/file-share presentation is central to the workflow, changes to VM state, OS updates, or restore processes that affect share registration or permissions can directly impact service availability, making this a high-leverage control point for both operations and security reviews [Data: Entities (524,523)]. ## Change-control and restore process gap implied by update + VM-restore linkage The timeline links a Windows update and an associated VM restore to the onset of the network-share problem, implying the update/restore process — and its post-restore verification steps — may lack sufficient validation for share/service availability after a restore [Data: Relationships (890); Entities (523)]. This pattern indicates a procedural or tooling gap in post-update/restore health checks for print-server services (SMB shares, queue registration) that should be addressed to prevent recurrence [Data: Relationships (883,891)]. ## People and communication: cross-team troubleshooting performed but formal runbook evidence not shown Records show Employee 20 was tasked to validate whether the issue was on SAP's side and was acknowledged in internal thanks from Employee 19, demonstrating collaborative troubleshooting across application and infrastructure teams during the incident [Data: Entities (517); Relationships (906,879)]. However, the documented fix (disable/re-enable of shares) appears to have been executed as an operational workaround rather than from a recorded, automated runbook in the dataset; the absence of a referenced runbook or automated remediation suggests opportunity to codify the steps for faster, lower-risk recovery in future events [Data: Relationships (891); Entities (521)].

All other employees in the same room can print without issue. The affected user receives the message “Access denied” when attempting to print. 02.12.25 09:03 Ticket created and assigned to Employee 27. 02.12.25 09:20 – Initial analysis Remote connection established with the affected user. Printer “MFP-Basel-01” is visible. When sending a print job, “Access denied” appears immediately. Print job is not displayed in the queue. 02.12.25 09:45 – Comparison with another user Test performed with colleague in the same office. Printing works without restriction. User account checked. Membership in location group “PRT-Basel” confirmed. 02.12.25 10:30 – Further review Comparison with “Print & Scanning Infrastructure Standard – Version 1.0”. It specifies: • Printer permissions are assigned exclusively via AD groups • No individual user permissions at queue level • NTFS and share permissions must be consistent Review of the printer queue on the print server shows: • AD group “PRT-Basel” has print permission • Additionally, there is an explicit entry for the affected user with “Deny” This entry originates from a previous test configuration. 02.12.25 11:10 – Test Explicit deny permission removed. User logged in again. Test print successful. 03.12.25 09:40 – Customer feedback Printing works normally again. Additional tests within the team successful. 03.12.25 10:21 – Ticket closure Dear Contact Person 20 The root cause was an explicit “Deny” permission on the printer queue for the affected user. No abnormalities observed. 12.11.25 14:40 – Customer feedback Printer is functioning normally again. Larger print jobs are also processed correctly. 12.11.25 14:52 – Ticket closure Dear Contact Person 15 The root cause was a missing network allowance between the print server and the newly configured printer VLAN at the Basel location. The configuration was adjusted according to the “Print & Scanning Infrastructure Standard”. The printer is fully reachable again. Kind regards Consulting Sample Company REPORT 15: # Basel Print Infrastructure — Print Server, MFP-BASEL-01 & Printer VLAN The community centers on centralized Windows print servers that host location-based printer queues and enforce queue-level permissions via Active Directory groups and Group Policy; physical printers (including MFP-BASEL-01) are deployed into a dedicated PRINTER VLAN that requires inter-VLAN routing and firewall/ACL rules so the print server and client workstations can reach them. An operational incident (TI-1622483) combined a misconfigured explicit Deny ACE on the MFP-BASEL-01 queue with recent network segmentation that initially prevented the print server from reaching the printer until routing/firewall rules were corrected; remediation removed the explicit Deny, corrected AD/GPO configuration and adjusted network rules according to the Print & Scanning Infrastructure Standard and Decision Tree guidance. Key artifacts that govern behavior and troubleshooting are the Print & Scanning Infrastructure Standard and an embedded Decision Tree which prescribe AD-group-based provisioning, GPO-based default printers, SMTP relay restrictions for printer VLANs, and a diagnostic checklist covering spooler, queue permissions and VLAN reachability. [Data: Entities (70,52,137,53,427); Relationships (112,119,704,222,695)] ## Canonical architecture: central Windows print servers + VLAN‑isolated printers, mapped by AD/GPO Printing is implemented as a centralized service: Windows print servers host queues and standardized drivers, and queues are mapped to users via AD groups and Group Policy (location-based queues and defaults). This is the foundational design documented in the Print & Scanning Infrastructure Standard and implemented via GPOs and provisioning processes. [Data: Entities (70,718,53,57); Relationships (112,92)] Because printers are placed in a dedicated PRINTER VLAN, reachability depends on inter-VLAN routing and firewall/ACL rules; the standard requires routing so the print server can reach the printers and instructs operators to validate VLAN reachability (ping) as part of diagnostics. This separation centralizes printer topology but creates a dependency between print services and network configuration that must be validated on every VLAN change. [Data: Entities (137,1280); Relationships (715,1312)] ## Incident root cause: overlapping queue-level DENY ACE plus network segmentation The production outage reported in TI-1622483 manifested as an affected user receiving immediate “Access denied” errors while other users' jobs either remained in the queue or disappeared without printing. Troubleshooting identified two correlated root causes: an explicit Deny ACE on the MFP-BASEL-01 queue that overrode group-based Allow permissions, and the printer’s move into a separate PRINTER VLAN that initially prevented the print server from reaching the device until routing/firewall rules were adjusted. Both issues were necessary to explain the mixed symptoms observed. [Data: Entities (73,52,71,137); Relationships (119,704)] This demonstrates a classic dual-failure mode where identity/ACL misconfiguration blocks a single principal while network segmentation blocks server-to-device delivery for other paths — both must be checked in parallel when jobs appear then disappear. [Data: Relationships (704,119)] ## Remediation followed standards: Deny removed, AD/GPO corrected, firewall/routing adjusted Resolution actions documented in the ticket included removal of the explicit Deny ACE on the printer queue, correction of AD group membership and forcing a Group Policy refresh so location-based defaults applied correctly, and adjustment of the firewall/routing rule to allow the print server to reach the PRINTER VLAN. The ticket closure explicitly cites configuration changes implemented according to the Print & Scanning Infrastructure Standard. These combined changes restored normal behavior and the customer confirmed functionality. [Data: Entities (427,73,53); Relationships (80,695)] The remediation path aligns to the Decision Tree’s prescribed sequence (permission checks, GPO refresh, VLAN reachability tests) and is therefore repeatable and auditable when executed against the standard. [Data: Entities (164); Relationships (1312,1311)] ## Operational decision-tree and diagnostics are present and were leveraged An operational Decision Tree and Use Cases document exists to drive consistent troubleshooting (checklist of spooler state, queue visibility, AD membership, VLAN reachability, paused queues, and test prints). The decision tree specifically includes steps for VLAN reachability/ping, paused-queue checks, and print‑job presence/absence to route diagnostics. This artifact was used to guide verification and is a key enabler of consistent incident handling. [Data: Entities (164); Relationships (1312,1311,1310)] Maintaining and enforcing use of this decision tree reduces mean time to repair by ensuring both identity/permission and network segments are validated early in the workflow. ## GPO/AD design fragility: overlapping or parallel groups produce incorrect default mappings Group Policy assigns location-based default printers by evaluating AD group membership; overlapping or parallel AD groups have previously caused the Zurich printer to be applied to Basel users until memberships and GPO targeting were corrected. The environment relies on single-purpose AD groups (no parallel groups) per the provisioning rules in the standard, and deviations here directly cause incorrect printer mapping and user experience issues. [Data: Entities (57,64); Relationships (92,88)] Recommendation: enforce strict group-naming and lifecycle controls, validate nested memberships during change windows, and include membership checks in the GPO deployment runbook to prevent cross-location mapping errors. ## SMTP/scan‑to‑mail compliance gap for new PRINTER VLAN The PRINTER VLAN’s IP range had not been added to the organization’s SMTP relay allow list when the VLAN was created, causing external SMTP relay attempts from devices in that VLAN to be denied until the range was whitelisted. The Print & Scanning Infrastructure Standard prohibits printers sending directly to the Internet and requires usage of a dedicated SMTP relay whose access list is maintained. This is a non-printing impact that affects scan‑to‑mail functionality and compliance with the standard. [Data: Entities (137,53); Relationships (222)] Network and change-management checklists must include updates to SMTP relay ACLs when introducing new device VLANs to avoid secondary outages impacting scan workflows. ## Monitoring and diagnostics gap: spooler state alone is insufficient — include queue ACL and VLAN checks The Print Spooler service on the server was observed to be active while print jobs were not reaching the printer; restarting the spooler can transiently change symptoms but does not correct queue‑level ACLs or network routing problems. The decision tree explicitly separates spooler health from queue status and VLAN reachability checks, indicating the need for multi-dimensional monitoring and alerting (service health + queue ACL anomalies + inter-VLAN reachability). [Data: Entities (159,429); Relationships (255,1312)] Operationally, alerts that only surface a running spooler but declining print completion rates should automatically trigger queue-ACL and VLAN-reachability checks as part of the incident playbook. ## Clear stakeholder boundaries but ad hoc coordination required: IT Infrastructure, Network Team, site contacts Document ownership and operational responsibilities are defined: IT Infrastructure authors and enforces the Print & Scanning Infrastructure Standard and provisions printers; the Network Team is responsible for VLAN configuration and firewall changes and was engaged to adjust the firewall rule that blocked server-to-printer traffic; the ticket lists a site contact who confirmed restoration. However, VLAN and SMTP ACL changes show that coordination between these groups must be formalized in the change process to prevent related failures. [Data: Entities (717,428,427); Relationships (712,222,695)] Recommendation: define explicit escalation and approval paths for changes that affect both identity/provisioning and network segmentation, include cross-team sign-off in the change template, and publish a short runbook for combined print-network incidents. REPORT 55: # TI-722511 — Financial Reports outage (AOS/IIS TLS cipher-suite change) Ticket TI-722511 documents a reproducible outage where users (EMEA finance) could not open Financial Reports in both UAT and PROD; the incident was triaged with a consulting partner and resolved by activating best-practice TLS cipher suites using IISCRYPTO and restarting AOS (and IIS) servers. Key infrastructure components implicated are AOS servers and IIS servers (TLS/cipher negotiation), TLS policy/configuration and Windows security updates as potential contributors; an internal JIRA (ICO261-183) from another region reported the same error, indicating a global or multi-region manifestation that requires coordinated configuration and testing across environments. Ownership and operational actions were recorded in portal notes (contact persons and consulting company involvement), and the ticket closure explicitly links the TLS/cipher change and server restarts to the remediation outcome. ## TI-722511 is a production-impacting, multi-environment incident resolved by TLS/cipher-suite changes and server restarts The ticket TI-722511 records reproducible errors accessing Financial Reports across UAT and PROD and documents closure text indicating that best-practice cipher suites were activated with IISCRYPTO and AOS servers were restarted as the remediation that resolved the issue. This linkage between the ticket, the tool (IISCRYPTO), the configuration change and the server restarts is explicitly documented in the closure notes and portal history [Data: Entities (0); Entities (18,20); Relationships (9,34,36)]. Because the ticket affects both UAT and PROD and includes symptom reports from EMEA finance users (report creators appearing as "unknown" and inability to open reports), this is a production-grade operational incident rather than a single-user problem; the ticket metadata and relationships show cross-environment impact and operator action items that are relevant to architecture and runbook updates [Data: Entities (0); Relationships (1,10)]. ## AOS and IIS servers are central operational dependencies for the reporting service AOS servers broker requests from user clients (browser/Report Designer) to backend reporting services and were explicitly restarted as part of the remediation, showing they are a required operational touchpoint when TLS/cipher changes are applied. The relationships document the dependency between AOS servers, users and TLS policy changes, and record that AOS restarts were necessary after cipher adjustments [Data: Entities (19,483,487); Relationships (796,798,36)]. IIS servers are also the target of IISCRYPTO changes and therefore form the TLS termination layer that directly affects client report access; the record linking IISCRYPTO to IIS servers and to the best-practice cipher-suite configuration confirms they are part of the change domain that produced the outage [Data: Entities (26,18,20); Relationships (35,34)]. ## TLS / cipher-suite configuration and Windows security updates are a plausible root-cause vector and must be part of diagnostics The dataset explicitly identifies TLS/cipher-suite configuration as a primary decision-tree item for multi-environment report failures and calls out that Windows security updates can alter available crypto libraries or TLS behavior, impacting negotiation and compatibility [Data: Entities (480,488); Relationships (803)]. Operationally this means any change to the TLS configuration (cipher suites or minimum TLS versions) or a cumulative OS/security patch may introduce incompatibilities between client/report-designer, IIS, AOS and backend services; the incident remediation (activating best-practice ciphers and restarting servers) and the diagnostic checklist in the records both point to these items as the first-line troubleshooting steps [Data: Entities (0,20,480,488); Relationships (9,803,36)]. ## The incident shows cross-regional recurrence — internal Jira ICO261-183 reports the same error An internal JIRA ticket (ICO261-183) opened by another internal team (Mexico) reported the same error, and the portal notes for TI-722511 reference that JIRA ticket, indicating the problem was observed outside a single operating region and should be treated as a global/tenant-level issue requiring coordinated remediation and communication [Data: Entities (17); Relationships (33); Entities (0); Relationships (0)]. This cross-reference implies the root cause is likely systemic (configuration or policy across environments) rather than an isolated host-level fault, elevating the need for a deployment-wide review of TLS/cipher policies, synchronized change windows, and shared runbooks to avoid reoccurrence [Data: Entities (17,0); Relationships (33,9)]. ## Consulting company acted as primary triage/coordination but operational ownership and escalation paths are fragmented Portal no-reply entries and the ticket relationships identify a consulting organization performing triage, posting portal updates and coordinating remediation activities for TI-722511, which indicates third-party involvement in operational execution [Data: Entities (24); Relationships (16)]. While external support can accelerate remediation, the records show multiple actors (customer contact, consulting company, internal teams, and referenced JIRA) are involved; this multiplicity increases coordination overhead and suggests the need to codify explicit escalation and handoff steps (who is authorized to change TLS settings, who validates post-change testing, and who owns cross-region communication) to reduce confusion during future incidents [Data: Entities (2,24,17); Relationships (1,16,33)]. ## The ticket closure documents a fix but there is limited explicit trace of pre- and post-change testing and rollback procedures Closure text states best-practice cipher suites were activated with IISCRYPTO and servers restarted, and this outcome is associated with resolution of the ticket, but the dataset does not include detailed pre-change compatibility testing results, a staged rollout record, or an explicit rollback plan linked to the change [Data: Entities (0,18,20); Relationships (9,34)]. For production TLS changes that can break client compatibility, the absence of documented staged testing or rollback guidance in the ticket and relationships represents an operational risk — future changes should include mandatory compatibility validation (Report Designer and browser tests across supported clients), versioned configuration snapshots, and an approved rollback procedure recorded in the change ticket before applying IISCRYPTO or similar tooling [Data: Entities (0,480,18); Relationships (9,34,35)]. ## Recommended immediate actions: formalize TLS change control, add AOS/IIS restart playbook, and update monitoring/alerts Given the documented remediation and dependencies, the highest-priority operational actions are: 1) formalize a TLS/cipher-suite change control process that enforces synchronized configuration across UAT and PROD and requires pre-change validation; 2) add a concise runbook for TLS changes that includes steps to use IISCRYPTO, how and when to restart IIS and AOS servers, and post-change verification for report access; and 3) tune monitoring/alerts to detect report designer failures, unknown report-owner symptoms, and TLS negotiation failures so teams can detect regressions quickly. These recommendations are directly derived from the incident facts that link TLS configuration, IISCRYPTO use, and server restarts to resolution [Data: Entities (0,18,20,19,26,480); Relationships (9,34,36,35,796)]. Implementing these controls will reduce time-to-detect and time-to-recover for similar incidents and make cross-team communication and escalation more predictable. ## Governance and long-term mitigation: standardize TLS baselines, document supported client profiles, and maintain a cross-region configuration registry The incident demonstrates the need for organizational governance around TLS baselines (approved cipher suites and minimum TLS versions) and explicit documentation of supported client versions for the Report Designer and browser clients. Records show the problem manifested across regions and environments and was tied to cipher-suite changes, reinforcing that a single source of truth for TLS configuration and supported client compatibility is required [Data: Entities (480,483); Relationships (803,796,36)]. A practical control is a configuration registry (or CMDB extension) that records TLS/cipher settings per environment and a pre-deployment verification checklist that gates any change; combine this with scheduled compatibility testing against a matrix of client versions to avoid breaking production users when adopting "best-practice" cryptographic settings that may remove older ciphers still required by legacy clients [Data: Entities (480,483); Relationships (36,796)]. ## Communication and escalation runbook: define stakeholder matrix and cross-reference internal tracking systems The records link the ticket to named customer contacts (Contact Person 7, Regina Jagsch), a consulting company, and an internal JIRA that reported the same error, indicating multiple stakeholders and tracking systems were in use during the incident [Data: Entities (2,11,24,17); Relationships (1,10,16,33)]. To streamline future incidents, create a short escalation runbook that maps roles (customer contact, platform-owner, consulting SPOC, security owner), primary communication channels, and which external/internal ticketing system must be updated (portal ticket and JIRA). Include templates for status updates and a requirement to escalate to platform engineering when TLS/cipher changes are proposed, ensuring a single coordinated change window and clear ownership for rollback and postmortem actions [Data: Entities (0,2,11,24,17); Relationships (1,10,16,33)].

The relationships document the dependency between AOS servers, users and TLS policy changes, and record that AOS restarts were necessary after cipher adjustments [Data: Entities (19,483,487); Relationships (796,798,36)]. IIS servers are also the target of IISCRYPTO changes and therefore form the TLS termination layer that directly affects client report access; the record linking IISCRYPTO to IIS servers and to the best-practice cipher-suite configuration confirms they are part of the change domain that produced the outage [Data: Entities (26,18,20); Relationships (35,34)]. ## TLS / cipher-suite configuration and Windows security updates are a plausible root-cause vector and must be part of diagnostics The dataset explicitly identifies TLS/cipher-suite configuration as a primary decision-tree item for multi-environment report failures and calls out that Windows security updates can alter available crypto libraries or TLS behavior, impacting negotiation and compatibility [Data: Entities (480,488); Relationships (803)]. Operationally this means any change to the TLS configuration (cipher suites or minimum TLS versions) or a cumulative OS/security patch may introduce incompatibilities between client/report-designer, IIS, AOS and backend services; the incident remediation (activating best-practice ciphers and restarting servers) and the diagnostic checklist in the records both point to these items as the first-line troubleshooting steps [Data: Entities (0,20,480,488); Relationships (9,803,36)]. ## The incident shows cross-regional recurrence — internal Jira ICO261-183 reports the same error An internal JIRA ticket (ICO261-183) opened by another internal team (Mexico) reported the same error, and the portal notes for TI-722511 reference that JIRA ticket, indicating the problem was observed outside a single operating region and should be treated as a global/tenant-level issue requiring coordinated remediation and communication [Data: Entities (17); Relationships (33); Entities (0); Relationships (0)]. This cross-reference implies the root cause is likely systemic (configuration or policy across environments) rather than an isolated host-level fault, elevating the need for a deployment-wide review of TLS/cipher policies, synchronized change windows, and shared runbooks to avoid reoccurrence [Data: Entities (17,0); Relationships (33,9)]. ## Consulting company acted as primary triage/coordination but operational ownership and escalation paths are fragmented Portal no-reply entries and the ticket relationships identify a consulting organization performing triage, posting portal updates and coordinating remediation activities for TI-722511, which indicates third-party involvement in operational execution [Data: Entities (24); Relationships (16)]. While external support can accelerate remediation, the records show multiple actors (customer contact, consulting company, internal teams, and referenced JIRA) are involved; this multiplicity increases coordination overhead and suggests the need to codify explicit escalation and handoff steps (who is authorized to change TLS settings, who validates post-change testing, and who owns cross-region communication) to reduce confusion during future incidents [Data: Entities (2,24,17); Relationships (1,16,33)]. ## The ticket closure documents a fix but there is limited explicit trace of pre- and post-change testing and rollback procedures Closure text states best-practice cipher suites were activated with IISCRYPTO and servers restarted, and this outcome is associated with resolution of the ticket, but the dataset does not include detailed pre-change compatibility testing results, a staged rollout record, or an explicit rollback plan linked to the change [Data: Entities (0,18,20); Relationships (9,34)]. For production TLS changes that can break client compatibility, the absence of documented staged testing or rollback guidance in the ticket and relationships represents an operational risk — future changes should include mandatory compatibility validation (Report Designer and browser tests across supported clients), versioned configuration snapshots, and an approved rollback procedure recorded in the change ticket before applying IISCRYPTO or similar tooling [Data: Entities (0,480,18); Relationships (9,34,35)]. ## Recommended immediate actions: formalize TLS change control, add AOS/IIS restart playbook, and update monitoring/alerts Given the documented remediation and dependencies, the highest-priority operational actions are: 1) formalize a TLS/cipher-suite change control process that enforces synchronized configuration across UAT and PROD and requires pre-change validation; 2) add a concise runbook for TLS changes that includes steps to use IISCRYPTO, how and when to restart IIS and AOS servers, and post-change verification for report access; and 3) tune monitoring/alerts to detect report designer failures, unknown report-owner symptoms, and TLS negotiation failures so teams can detect regressions quickly. These recommendations are directly derived from the incident facts that link TLS configuration, IISCRYPTO use, and server restarts to resolution [Data: Entities (0,18,20,19,26,480); Relationships (9,34,36,35,796)]. Implementing these controls will reduce time-to-detect and time-to-recover for similar incidents and make cross-team communication and escalation more predictable. ## Governance and long-term mitigation: standardize TLS baselines, document supported client profiles, and maintain a cross-region configuration registry The incident demonstrates the need for organizational governance around TLS baselines (approved cipher suites and minimum TLS versions) and explicit documentation of supported client versions for the Report Designer and browser clients. Records show the problem manifested across regions and environments and was tied to cipher-suite changes, reinforcing that a single source of truth for TLS configuration and supported client compatibility is required [Data: Entities (480,483); Relationships (803,796,36)]. A practical control is a configuration registry (or CMDB extension) that records TLS/cipher settings per environment and a pre-deployment verification checklist that gates any change; combine this with scheduled compatibility testing against a matrix of client versions to avoid breaking production users when adopting "best-practice" cryptographic settings that may remove older ciphers still required by legacy clients [Data: Entities (480,483); Relationships (36,796)]. ## Communication and escalation runbook: define stakeholder matrix and cross-reference internal tracking systems The records link the ticket to named customer contacts (Contact Person 7, Regina Jagsch), a consulting company, and an internal JIRA that reported the same error, indicating multiple stakeholders and tracking systems were in use during the incident [Data: Entities (2,11,24,17); Relationships (1,10,16,33)]. To streamline future incidents, create a short escalation runbook that maps roles (customer contact, platform-owner, consulting SPOC, security owner), primary communication channels, and which external/internal ticketing system must be updated (portal ticket and JIRA). Include templates for status updates and a requirement to escalate to platform engineering when TLS/cipher changes are proposed, ensuring a single coordinated change window and clear ownership for rollback and postmortem actions [Data: Entities (0,2,11,24,17); Relationships (1,10,16,33)]. REPORT 25: # NDES / AD CS Certificate Enrollment Community (NDES, Issuing CA, Templates, Devices) This community centers on the Network Device Enrollment Service (NDES) as the operational bridge that automates certificate issuance from Active Directory Certificate Services (AD CS) to target devices (network appliances and managed endpoints). NDES depends directly on AD CS (Issuing CA and certificate templates) and on AD CS post-configuration steps being completed; it also integrates with Intune and targets both network devices and managed endpoints for automated enrollment. The Issuing CA and Certificate Templates are critical configuration points: misconfigured templates or incorrect CA selection commonly cause issuance failures, while Root CA and Issuing CA operational problems (expiry or mismanagement) break certificate validation across consumers. Key operational failure modes called out in the corpus include missing IIS components or NDES service account misconfiguration, incomplete AD CS post-configuration, and template/CA misconfiguration; the decision-tree used for troubleshooting explicitly gates issuance testing on AD CS post-configuration and template correctness. [Data: Entities (360, 364, 365, 370, 369, 375, 376, 363), Relationships (584, 589, 590, 592, 594, 595, 601, 602)] ## NDES is the central operational component connecting AD CS to device consumers NDES functions as the active enrollment service that requests certificates from AD CS and provisions them to devices and managed endpoints; this makes it the focal point for certificate-based device authentication in the environment [Data: Entities (360), Relationships (584, 590, 595)]. Because NDES both initiates requests (to the Issuing CA using configured templates) and delivers certificates to targets (network devices and managed endpoints), failures in NDES manifest as downstream authentication and connectivity problems for many device types—making timely detection and remediation critical for device management and network security posture [Data: Relationships (589, 594, 595)]. ## Common NDES failure modes are documented and actionable The NDES description explicitly lists operational failure modes including inability to start the service, missing IIS components, service-account misconfiguration, AD CS post-configuration errors, and broken communication between NDES and the CA; these are primary troubleshooting paths to investigate when issuance fails [Data: Entities (360)]. The decision-tree logic in the blueprint enforces verification steps (for example, checking AD CS post-configuration) before attempting issuance tests, which both documents these frequent failure causes and provides an operational sequence for root-cause isolation [Data: Entities (360), Relationships (592)]. ## Certificate template misconfiguration is a frequent, high-impact root cause Certificate templates define key usage, validity, and subject fields for issued certificates and are explicitly called out as a common cause of failed automated issuance when misconfigured; the blueprint recommends documentation and regular review of templates to prevent these issues [Data: Entities (365)]. Operationally, templates are consumed by the Issuing CA to produce end-entity certificates; if templates are incorrect on the CA side or selected incorrectly by NDES, issuance will fail or produce certificates incompatible with device expectations, causing authentication errors across the environment [Data: Relationships (602, 589)]. ## CA hierarchy (Root CA → Issuing CA) is a single point of trust and failure The Root CA is the trust anchor that signs the Issuing CA certificate; therefore issues at the Root CA or Issuing CA (expiry, mismanagement, incorrect chaining) can break certificate validation for all issued certificates and impact broad sets of devices and services [Data: Entities (363, 364), Relationships (601)]. Because NDES must be configured to use the correct Issuing CA, any mismatch between NDES configuration and the operational Issuing CA (or problems with the CA chain) will prevent successful enrollment and cause widespread authentication failures until corrected [Data: Relationships (584, 602)]. ## Network devices and managed endpoints are critical targets and verification points The blueprint identifies network appliances (routers, switches, firewalls, VPN gateways) and Intune-managed endpoints as primary recipients of NDES-issued certificates; these device classes should be part of any enrollment verification and post-change validation tests [Data: Entities (375, 376, 370), Relationships (594, 595, 590)]. Because successful issuance is verified by testing enrollment on targeted devices as the final decision-tree step, operational runbooks and monitoring should include sample enrollments against representative device models and managed endpoint profiles to detect regressions early [Data: Relationships (590)]. ## AD CS post-configuration is a gating dependency for NDES operation The corpus explicitly states that AD CS post-configuration (registration of NDES components, enabling required templates, selecting the correct Issuing CA) must be completed before proceeding to issuance tests; the decision tree checks this as a distinct step, making it a documented dependency that must be verified during deployments and troubleshooting [Data: Entities (369), Relationships (592)]. Operational processes should therefore include a checklist for AD CS post-configuration sign-off as a prerequisite for NDES activation and for any change that affects templates or CA selection to avoid wasted troubleshooting effort and false negatives during enrollment testing [Data: Entities (369), Relationships (592)]. ## Recommended operational controls: template governance, CA lifecycle management, and NDES platform hardening The dataset highlights template documentation and regular review as mitigations against issuance failures; implementing template governance (versioning, approvals, change logging) directly addresses a repeatedly-cited root cause [Data: Entities (365)]. Similarly, because Root CA and Issuing CA issues break validation widely, organizations need CA lifecycle controls (expiry monitoring, dedicated owners, offline Root CA handling procedures) to reduce systemic risk; these controls are supported by the Root CA and Issuing CA descriptions that call out expiry and mismanagement as service-impacting events [Data: Entities (363, 364), Relationships (601)]. Finally, hardening and operational checks for NDES—ensuring correct IIS components, validating the NDES service account, and maintaining reliable connectivity between NDES and the Issuing CA—map directly to the NDES failure modes and should be codified in installation and runbook procedures [Data: Entities (360)]. ## Suggested stakeholder/escalation pathway derived from dependency graph The relationship map shows NDES depending on AD CS (Issuing CA and templates) and provisioning certificates to devices, which implies a practical escalation sequence: NDES operations (first responder) → Issuing CA/AD CS administrators (certificate/template investigations) → Root CA owners (if trust-chain/expiry issues) → device owners (network or endpoint teams) for remediation and verification [Data: Entities (360, 364, 363, 370), Relationships (584, 601, 590)]. Making this path explicit in runbooks will shorten mean-time-to-resolution by directing incidents to the correct technical owner quickly—particularly for issues like template misconfiguration or CA chain problems that require CA-side changes rather than device-side fixes [Data: Relationships (589, 602, 590)]. REPORT 53: # Financial Reporting Service — Report Designer and AOS (001-SRV-AOS72/73) The community centers on REPORT DESIGNER (the client/editor) and the FINANCIAL REPORTING SERVICE runtime, which depend on AOS application servers (notably 001-SRV-AOS72 and 001-SRV-AOS73), the Data Mart/FRDB, SQL, and network/TLS/authentication plumbing. Users experience globally slow startup and rendering, intermittent "Report cannot be found" errors, and lost author metadata (authors shown as "unknown"). Remediation activity included FRDB recreation and Data Mart resets in the D365FO_INTE environment and a Microsoft support ticket was opened to investigate the global failures. Observations point to a mix of node-specific application-server degradation (72 vs 73), dependency failures affecting metadata lookup (service account/TLS/permissions), and potential network/latency factors that together produce the end-user symptoms. ## REPORT DESIGNER is tightly coupled to multiple backend dependencies (AOS, Data Mart/FRDB, SQL, and TLS/auth) and exhibits systemic symptoms. REPORT DESIGNER’s correct operation depends on successful TLS negotiation, service-account authentication/authorization, and reliable connectivity to AOS, the Data Mart/FRDB, and SQL. The symptom set reported (global slowness, "Report cannot be found", and missing author metadata) aligns with failures in one or more of those dependencies rather than purely a local client issue. This dependency surface makes the client sensitive to infrastructure, identity/auth, and database issues at multiple layers. The source material explicitly cites TLS compatibility, service account authentication, and reachability to AOS/Data Mart/SQL as required dependencies and diagnostic vectors for the observed problems. This implies remediation must treat authentication, certificate/TLS compatibility, and backend reachability as first-class troubleshooting targets rather than focusing solely on client-side tuning or UI fixes. [Data: Entities (34,477); Relationships (791,785)] ## Node-specific issue: 001-SRV-AOS72 behaves as a diagnostic fast-path while 001-SRV-AOS73 is sluggish — indicating a per-node degradation or configuration drift. When REPORT DESIGNER is launched directly on 001-SRV-AOS72 the client is performant, while normal (remote) access that routes through the service tier encounters global slowness and degraded behavior. This divergence between twin siblings suggests either a node-specific configuration, caching, or connectivity difference on 001-SRV-AOS73 (or the network path to it) rather than an application-wide bug. Relationships and entity descriptions identify 001-SRV-AOS72 as the node that returns acceptable performance and 001-SRV-AOS73 as the sluggish sibling implicated in degraded end-user experience; the pair are explicitly described as sibling application servers in the same tier. Investigation should therefore include node configuration comparison, local caches, certificate stores, service-account context, and network path/middleware (load balancer or firewall) differences between the two servers. [Data: Entities (35,36); Relationships (61,55)] ## Author metadata loss points to metadata store access or permission issues affecting existing reports. Reports are showing authors as "unknown", which is consistent with metadata being inaccessible or lost from the source store (or being present but unreadable due to permission/identity failures). The presence of a "PREVIOUS REPORT CREATORS" group and the explicit relationship noting these creators are now displayed as unknown indicate that author metadata either wasn’t migrated, became orphaned, or cannot be read by the reporting runtime due to permission or authentication issues. Restoring or validating metadata read paths requires validating the service account(s) used by the Financial Reporting Service and Report Designer have read access to metadata stores, and confirming that any database/schema changes (including FRDB recreation) preserved or remapped author identity attributes. Audit the metadata access patterns and permission mappings to determine whether the metadata still exists but is blocked, or was removed during prior maintenance. [Data: Entities (45,34); Relationships (58)] ## FRDB recreation and Data Mart resets in D365FO_INTE were used as remediation; this suggests data-store corruption or misalignment was suspected/observed. The FRDB instance in the D365FO_INTE environment was recreated by an engineer as part of remediation steps and the environment was used to manage FRDB/Data Mart artifacts. Recreating FRDB indicates the remediation team suspected the database schema or contents were out-of-sync or corrupted for reporting consumption, and used the integration/test environment to rebuild report data artifacts. While FRDB recreation can resolve schema or corruption issues, it also risks metadata loss or identity remapping if not coordinated with metadata and author attribution preservation. Any further remediation should include metadata validation, backups, and a reconciliation step to ensure recreated FRDB instances contain the expected author and metadata references or that mappings are restored. [Data: Entities (38,37); Relationships (60,62)] ## Network latency is an acknowledged contributing factor and must be ruled in/out as part of root-cause analysis. Network latency is explicitly included in the decision tree as a cause for extreme startup slowness of Report Designer. Given the multi-tier traffic pattern (client → Reporting runtime → AOS → Data Mart/SQL) even modest latency or intermittent path degradation can amplify startup delays and timeouts, worsening perceived performance. Investigations should collect latency and packet-path telemetry from representative clients to each app server (including 001-SRV-AOS72/73), and from app servers to Data Mart/SQL. Correlate latency spikes or retransmissions with user-reported slowdowns and with the times FRDB resets or configuration changes occurred to identify whether network factors coincidentally or causally contributed to the incidents. [Data: Entities (490,34); Relationships (791)] ## Microsoft support engagement indicates the issue crosses product boundaries and requires coordinated vendor troubleshooting alongside internal remediation. A Microsoft support ticket was opened specifically to investigate the global reporting/Report Designer failures and related symptoms. Escalating to Microsoft suggests the internal team reached a boundary where vendor insights (for the reporting product/runtime, TLS negotiation behaviors, or AOS interactions) were needed, and that the incident may involve platform-level behavior or documented product bugs. This necessitates a clear escalation and evidence package (repro steps, diagnostic logs from 72/73, TLS traces, service-account access failures, FRDB snapshots) to reduce time-to-resolution with the vendor. Maintain a central incident bundle and a named internal owner to coordinate evidence collection and vendor interactions to avoid duplicated effort and message fragmentation. [Data: Entities (34,477); Relationships (53)] ## Operational exposure: lack of clear ownership, cross-team handoffs, and potential for repeat incidents — prioritize dependency mapping, runbooks, and governance changes. The dataset shows multiple remediation actions (FRDB recreation, Data Mart reset), a Microsoft ticket, node-specific performance divergence, and metadata loss — a pattern that indicates gaps in runbook completeness, dependency visibility, and governance for change actions affecting reporting. Without a documented and practiced change plan and stakeholder matrix, FRDB/metadata changes risk recurrence or unintended data loss. Priority actions should include (1) an explicit dependency map linking REPORT DESIGNER → Financial Reporting Service → AOS nodes → Data Mart/FRDB → SQL → network/TLS/auth, (2) a stakeholder matrix naming owners for each layer (app, infra, DB, network, identity) and an escalation path (including vendor), (3) a rollback-capable change plan and runbook for FRDB/Data Mart work that preserves/validates metadata and author mappings, and (4) monitoring/alert tuning for node-level performance divergence (001-SRV-AOS72 vs 73). Implementing these will reduce mean time to detect and remediate recurrence. [Data: Entities (38,490); Relationships (62,53,55)]
